content.text:
read docs/research/06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md carefully. we tried to implement @openspec/changes/outline-decorations/proposal.mdand failed. I'm not sure whether it was just a bad attempt and it should be retried with the same general approach, or whether this is a dead end and we need to get back to the drawing board. I want you to to the analysis and keep an open mind for potential alternatives, revisiting even the design choices made before. do additional code digging on this particular topic in all of the open source outliners (e.g. Logseq, outl) and alternative outline/workflowy Obsidian plugins that handle tree-shaped editing. who do they achieve their results. what can we learn from that. how does this update our assessment of the feasibility. 
let's consider which experiments we need to try prototyping to figure out more and get more reliable plan for the next iteration.
I'll start by reading the postmortem and all the related design artifacts to understand what was attempted, then dig into the actual code and research external outliner implementations.

1	# Postmortem: the `outline-decorations` change (2026-07-13)
2	
3	**Verdict: failed experiment.** After three full redesign cycles, extensive automated
4	testing (198 unit tests, 33 e2e tests, all green), and multiple rounds of screenshot
5	verification, the feature was still visibly broken in real vault use: marker size
6	scaling with heading font-size, inconsistent indentation, wrong continuation-line
7	indentation, markers overlapping native bullets, misplaced guide lines. This document
8	records what was tried, why each attempt failed, and — the more important part — why
9	the testing approach used throughout kept producing false confidence. It's written for
10	whoever plans the next iteration (possibly a future session of the author of this one).
11	
12	## What the change was trying to do
13	
14	Give outline mode a visual language: headings, paragraphs, list items, and code/table/
15	quote/callout blocks should all read as nodes in one tree, with indentation that reflects
16	tree depth (not raw markdown encoding) and a marker prominent enough to make the
17	structure legible at a glance — motivated by a dev-vault finding that flat, paragraph-
18	heavy documents gave no visual signal that outline mode was even active. See
19	[04-open-questions.md](04-open-questions.md)'s "Visual layer is now the testability
20	bottleneck" note and the archived `outline-decorations` OpenSpec change for the original
21	ask.
22	
23	## Chronological account
24	
25	### Attempt 1: paragraph-only decoration, guide lines via fixed-position CSS
26	
27	Scope: only paragraph nodes got a `::before` marker; a separate `::before` line drew a
28	vertical "guide" at a fixed screen offset. List items, headings, and atoms were left
29	completely untouched.
30	
31	Shipped, all tasks marked done, verified via synthetic e2e fixtures (clean headings/
32	lists/paragraphs, no checkboxes) and two screenshots. Two real bugs were caught during
33	that verification round (both by screenshot, not by the DOM-attribute e2e assertions,
34	which passed the whole time):
35	
36	- The `padding-left` CSS rule needed a more specific selector *and* `!important` to take
37	  effect at all — a plain `.to-node-line { padding-left: ... }` was silently overridden
38	  by Obsidian's own later, equal-specificity `.cm-line` rules, even on lines with no
39	  competing native padding of their own.
40	- The rule read `var(--list-indent, 2em)` for the per-depth unit. CSS custom properties
41	  resolve at the point of *use*, not declaration — so the `em` resolved against each
42	  line's own font-size, and a heading (larger font) indented more per depth-level than a
43	  paragraph at the identical tree depth. Fixed with a plugin-local fixed `rem` constant.
44	
45	Both fixes were verified by re-screenshotting the *same two synthetic fixtures* and
46	declared done. Nothing with checkboxes, real nesting, or code blocks was tried yet.
47	
48	### User report: broken in the real vault
49	
50	Two problems, both invisible in the synthetic fixtures:
51	
52	1. `styles.css` wasn't even loading — `vault:install` symlinked `manifest.json` and
53	   `main.js` but not `styles.css`. Fixed, but this alone should have been a signal that
54	   the verification loop (screenshot the sandboxed e2e vault) was disconnected from what
55	   the user was actually running.
56	2. Once loaded: indentation "off, sometimes negative"; guide lines "all over the place,"
57	   including apparently when outline mode was off.
58	
59	### Attempt 2: unilateral pivot to "leave list items alone entirely"
60	
61	Root cause of "negative" indentation: Obsidian pairs a negative `text-indent` with
62	`padding-left` to hang a list item's bullet outside the text column. The `!important`
63	override touched only `padding-left`, leaving the native (smaller, per-raw-level)
64	`text-indent` in place — the mismatched pair put content in an arbitrary, sometimes
65	overlapping position. The guide line's fixed screen offset (attempt 1) also never scaled
66	with depth, so at real nesting it landed nowhere near the actual text.
67	
68	Response: stopped decorating list items, headings, and atoms altogether — only
69	paragraphs got padding or a marker — and dropped the guide-line feature entirely.
70	
71	**This was the first process failure of the session**: a scope reduction (dropping the
72	whole point of the change — one visual language across kinds) made and shipped without
73	checking with the user first. Called out directly: *"how come you decided to change the
74	design mid-implementation and didn't even stop to check with me?"*
75	
76	### User rejects the narrowed scope; redirects to a specific reference
77	
78	Explicit requirements: indentation must be *consistent relative to text* across every
79	block type; a *prominent, uniform "fat bullet"* marker (Logseq-style) across kinds, with
80	per-kind glyph variation an acceptable later refinement, not a blocker. Pointed at
81	`obsidian-outliner`'s `BetterListsStyles`/Vertical Indentation Lines as a concrete
82	reference implementation to study before writing more code.
83	
84	Research via DeepWiki on `vslinko/obsidian-outliner` surfaced the actual technique, which
85	this session had not been using:
86	
87	- Guide lines are **not** CSS. A `ViewPlugin` calls `view.coordsAtPos()`/`lineBlockAt()`
88	  to read *already-rendered* pixel positions (after Obsidian's own list math has run),
89	  then draws absolutely-positioned overlay `<div>`s at those measured coordinates. It
90	  never fights the box model — it measures the result and draws on top of it.
91	- Bullet restyling targets the **existing** native bullet DOM element
92	  (`.list-bullet::after`) rather than adding a new one, and is list-only — the plugin
93	  never needs to make a heading or paragraph imitate a list bullet, because vanilla
94	  Obsidian never indents those regardless of depth. That "make non-list kinds match list
95	  indentation" problem is unique to this project's universal-tree model; there was no
96	  existing-plugin precedent to lift for it.
97	- Its own native indent-guide dashes are explicitly disabled (`content: none` on
98	  `.cm-indent::before`) via a mode class when its custom guides are on, to avoid double
99	  rendering.
100	
101	Given the choice between "make everything match native list styling" and "make list
102	styling match everything else" (author's framing), the answer was explicit: unify by
103	owning the scheme, not by trying to reverse-engineer Obsidian's per-theme list metrics.
104	
105	### The `!important` warning already on file
106	
107	`docs/research/02-obsidian-plugin-landscape.md` documents a case study
108	(`workflowy-style-outline`) whose from-scratch UI shipped **~890 uses of `!important`**
109	fighting Obsidian's own CSS, flagged as the majority of its community-scorecard lint
110	violations. This was already in the project's own research before this session started
111	and should have been weighed *before* reaching for `!important` at all, not discovered
112	as a justification after the fact. It turned out to matter far sooner than the "add a
113	settings UI later" scale the case study implies: this session needed `!important` for
114	literally three properties (`padding-left`, `margin-left`, `text-indent`) and still
115	produced a chain of regressions.
116	
117	### Attempt 3: unify all kinds via Obsidian's own hang mechanism
118	
119	Design: instead of fighting the `text-indent`/`padding-left` pair, own both halves
120	together, uniformly, for every kind — `padding-left: depth × unit`,
121	`text-indent: -1 × hang`, applied as one coherent rule to every decorated line (not just
122	paragraphs). A synthetic `::before` marker on kinds without a native one rides the same
123	hang naturally (pseudo-elements participate in `text-indent`'s first-line shift, so no
124	absolute-position math needed there). List items keep their native marker glyph and
125	position; only its `font-size`/`font-weight` are touched.
126	
127	First verification round found nothing wrong — because it was run against a stale
128	plugin bundle. **`npm run build` only runs `tsc --noEmit`; it does not invoke esbuild.**
129	The actual `main.js` Obsidian loads still had the previous (paragraph-only) code. This
130	was caught only by directly inspecting computed styles and noticing list items carried
131	none of the new CSS classes at all — a second, independent build-pipeline mistake
132	(the same category of "test wasn't actually testing the current code" failure, just at
133	the build layer instead of the vault layer).
134	
135	After an actual rebuild, computed-style checks showed correct, consistent per-depth
136	padding (0/24/48/72px) across headings, paragraphs, and list items. Screenshots of a
137	mixed journal fixture (checkboxes, nested lists) looked right — except code blocks
138	still didn't visibly indent.
139	
140	**Root cause**: `padding-left` shifts an element's own *content*; it never moves the
141	element's own border/background box. Invisible for plain text (no visible box to reveal
142	the gap), but a code fence renders a background box whose edges are unaffected by
143	padding — confirmed via `getBoundingClientRect()`: the background box's `left` was
144	identical to its container's `left` regardless of the line's computed `padding-left`.
145	Fix: atoms (code/table/quote/callout/html/hr) get `margin-left` instead, which actually
146	moves the box.
147	
148	### Attempt 3b: unifying bullet *shape*, not just size
149	
150	Restyling the native bullet glyph in place (bigger font-size on the existing "-"
151	character) left bullets looking like bold dashes next to round "●" markers on paragraphs
152	and headings — a shape mismatch. Attempted fix: hide the native dash
153	(`color: transparent`) and draw a matching "●" via `::after`, positioned absolutely.
154	
155	This collided with a native Obsidian element never previously accounted for: the
156	**fold-indicator** (collapse chevron), rendered for any list item that has children. Its
157	wrapper element has zero width in normal flow, but its child icon (~31px) visually
158	extends past that zero-width anchor regardless of the surrounding indentation scheme —
159	so it always intrudes on whatever renders immediately after it. Verified by directly
160	measuring both elements' `getBoundingClientRect()`s and confirming the ~9px overlap
161	persisted even after changing the hang unit — because the fold-indicator's icon size is
162	independent of `--to-hang`; shrinking the hang just moved both anchors together, not
163	their relative overlap.
164	
165	Attempted fix: a fixed `left` offset on the `::after` dot to clear the fold-indicator.
166	This **made it worse** — the offset needed to clear the icon pushed the dot far enough
167	right to overlap the item's own text (visibly cutting off the first letter: "users"
168	rendered as "●sers"). The dot and the text both anchor near the same point; a flat pixel
169	offset that clears one native element pushes into the next thing in flow, because the
170	underlying mechanism (inline flow position, not independent absolute placement) doesn't
171	allow moving one without the other.
172	
173	Reverted to the safer, non-repositioning version: resize the *existing* glyph in place,
174	touch nothing about its position. This can't collide with the fold-indicator or overlap
175	text because it never moves anything — it accepts "bullets read as bold dashes, not
176	matching dots" as a known, deferred cosmetic gap (which the user had already flagged as
177	acceptable to defer, distinct from the indentation-consistency requirement which was
178	not negotiable).
179	
180	### Final user report: still broken
181	
182	Despite the above — full green test suite, multiple rounds of screenshot verification
183	against increasingly realistic fixtures (checkboxes, deep nesting, mixed ordered/bullet
184	lists, code fences) — real use in the actual vault still showed:
185	
186	- **Bullets get bigger depending on the heading size.** A real, self-evident bug this
187	  session never caught: the marker's `font-size` was set in `em` (`0.6em` for the
188	  synthetic marker, `1.15em` for the resized native glyph) — the *exact same class of
189	  mistake* already identified and fixed for *indentation* (em resolves against each
190	  line's own font-size) was never applied to the *marker size* rule. A heading's larger
191	  base font inflates its marker proportionally; a paragraph's or a deeply nested list
192	  item's marker stays small. This should have been caught by the same reasoning that
193	  caught the first em/rem bug — it wasn't, because that fix was verified narrowly
194	  (indentation values only) rather than by re-auditing every other `em`-based rule in
195	  the same stylesheet for the same class of bug.
196	- **Indentation still "all over the place"** in real use, despite computed-style checks
197	  showing correct, consistent depth-based padding in the tested fixtures. Either the
198	  verified fixtures didn't cover the actual failure modes present in a real, long-lived
199	  vault (different note shapes, different nesting patterns, interaction with the user's
200	  actual theme), or there are further interactions with Obsidian's own CSS not yet
201	  identified.
202	- **List node continuations (Shift+Enter multi-line nodes) don't get the right
203	  indentation.** Never actually screenshotted or visually checked — the implementation
204	  assumption (continuation lines get the same `padding-left` as their node's first line,
205	  just without the `text-indent` hang) was asserted in code and in the unit tests'
206	  line-fact computation, but never verified against a real rendered multi-line list item
207	  or paragraph. A real, plausible gap in coverage, not a confirmed root cause.
208	- **List markers drawn on top of the dashes.** This directly contradicts the last known
209	  implementation state (resize-in-place, no overlay `::after`) and was never
210	  independently investigated before this document was written — the two live
211	  possibilities are (a) the user was testing against a stale build (this session's
212	  build pipeline had already caused two prior false-negative verification rounds for
213	  exactly this reason), or (b) a real decoration-application bug — e.g., stale/duplicate
214	  entries surviving across a `DecorationSet` recompute or a toggle-off/on cycle — that
215	  was never looked into.
216	- **Guide lines completely misplaced.** The vertical guide-line feature was dropped
217	  after attempt 2 and never reintroduced in this session's final implementation, so this
218	  report likely refers to the marker/indentation confusion above rather than a literal
219	  guide line — but it wasn't reconciled with the user before this document was written.
220	
221	## Why the testing approach kept producing false confidence
222	
223	This is the most important section for planning the next attempt — the bugs above are
224	specific to a CSS-override strategy, but the *verification gaps* are a repeatable
225	pattern that will recur under a different implementation strategy too if not addressed
226	directly.
227	
228	1. **DOM-attribute assertions test that code ran, not that it looks right.** The e2e
229	   suite checked CSS class names and the `--to-depth` custom property were present and
230	   correctly valued — and passed at every stage, including stages where the rendered
231	   result was visibly broken. Presence of an attribute says nothing about the resulting
232	   visual layout once cascade, specificity, and Obsidian's own rules are in the mix.
233	
234	2. **Screenshots caught real bugs — but the screenshot fixtures were never adversarial
235	   enough, and were checked for the *specific* thing being fixed, not for regressions
236	   elsewhere.** Each round screenshotted a fixture built to demonstrate the fix just
237	   made, not a fixed, growing regression corpus covering every previously-verified
238	   scenario. A fix for fold-indicator clearance was checked against fold-indicator
239	   clearance; it was not cross-checked against the earlier "does text stay readable"
240	   scenario, so the text-overlap regression it introduced wasn't caught until a
241	   dedicated screenshot of that specific case.
242	
243	3. **Marker *size* was never scrutinized, only marker *presence/shape/position*.** Every
244	   screenshot taken in this session had headings and paragraphs with visibly different
245	   marker sizes sitting right next to each other — the data needed to catch the em/rem
246	   marker bug was in nearly every screenshot from attempt 3 onward. It wasn't caught
247	   because the reviewer (this session) was looking for "is there a marker, is it round,
248	   is it positioned correctly," not "is it the same size everywhere."
249	
250	4. **The build pipeline was a repeat source of false negatives.** `npm run build`
251	   (`tsc --noEmit`) does not rebuild `main.js`; only `npm run build:plugin` (esbuild)
252	   does. At least twice in this session, CSS/behavior was "verified" against a stale
253	   bundle, producing a confident but wrong read of the state (most dramatically: an
254	   entire verification round concluding "list items get zero decoration" when the real
255	   bug was simply that the test was running old code).
256	
257	5. **The sandboxed e2e vault and hand-built synthetic fixtures were not the real
258	   vault.** Real bugs (styles.css not loading at all; the fold-indicator collision,
259	   which only appears on list items with children; the marker-size bug, which only
260	   shows up when a heading and a paragraph are visible together) either didn't
261	   reproduce in the synthetic fixtures at all, or reproduced but weren't looked for.
262	   Testing exclusively against hand-picked, already-passing-shaped fixtures — rather
263	   than the user's actual notes, or a fixture corpus deliberately designed to be
264	   adversarial — kept validating the wrong thing.
265	
266	6. **A fully green automated test suite (198 unit + 33 e2e) was maintained by updating
267	   the tests to match whatever the current implementation did**, rather than encoding
268	   independent, fixed visual-correctness invariants that would fail if the
269	   implementation regressed. This means "all tests pass" never actually functioned as
270	   the safety net it appeared to be — it confirmed the code was internally consistent
271	   with itself, not that it was correct.
272	
273	## Carried-forward technical findings (implementation-agnostic)
274	
275	These hold regardless of what mechanism the next attempt uses:
276	
277	- `padding-left` moves content, never an element's own border/background box; anything
278	  with visible chrome (code fences, tables, callouts, quotes) needs `margin-left` or an
279	  equivalent box-moving property instead.
280	- Obsidian's native list hanging-indent is a `text-indent`(negative)+`padding-left`
281	  *pair*; if either half is overridden, both must be, together, deliberately, or the
282	  hang math breaks in ways that look like arbitrary misalignment.
283	- CSS custom properties resolve `em`/relative units at the point of *use*. Any rule
284	  meant to produce a *uniform* value across elements with different font-sizes (headings
285	  vs. body text) needs a `rem` (root-relative) or otherwise absolute unit — for every
286	  property that needs to be uniform, not just the one that was noticed first (this
287	  session fixed it for indentation and missed it for marker size in the same
288	  stylesheet).
289	- Obsidian's own `.cm-line` styles need higher specificity and often `!important` to
290	  override at all — confirmed repeatedly, not a one-off.
291	- The fold-indicator (collapse chevron) is present on any list item with children, is
292	  positioned at the same anchor as adjacent inline content, and extends a fixed pixel
293	  amount past that anchor independent of any custom indent scheme — a real collision
294	  hazard for any custom marker/overlay positioned near a list item's start.
295	- `obsidian-outliner`'s proven approach for guide lines is DOM measurement
296	  (`coordsAtPos`/`lineBlockAt`) plus absolutely-positioned overlay elements, not CSS
297	  padding/pseudo-element tricks — this session never actually implemented that approach
298	  despite researching it, and went with a CSS-only strategy throughout attempt 3, which
299	  is arguably the root cause of most of the fragility described above.
300	- This project's own prior research (`02-obsidian-plugin-landscape.md`) already flagged
301	  the risk of a CSS-override-heavy strategy (the workflowy-style-outline case study,
302	  ~890 `!important` uses) before this change was ever proposed. That warning was
303	  available and was not weighed heavily enough before committing to the CSS-override
304	  strategy this session used throughout.
305	
306	## Open questions for the next iteration
307	
308	- Should guide lines and markers be implemented via DOM measurement + overlay elements
309	  (obsidian-outliner's proven pattern) instead of CSS padding/margin/text-indent
310	  overrides? This session never actually tried that path for anything beyond bullet
311	  glyph restyling.
312	- Is visual unification across encodings (headings/lists/paragraphs all reading as one
313	  indentation language) achievable at all without either (a) fully replacing native
314	  rendering for every kind — the path this project's own research already flagged as
315	  scorecard-risky — or (b) a measurement-based approach robust enough to survive
316	  arbitrary themes and fold-indicator-style native chrome this session didn't fully
317	  catalog?
318	- Is there a fundamentally different way to signal "this is one tree" that doesn't
319	  require in-place chrome matching at all — e.g., a structural overview pane, fold/zoom
320	  affordances, or something else entirely?
321	- What would a *real* verification protocol look like for this class of change — one
322	  that doesn't repeat the false-confidence patterns above? At minimum: screenshot a
323	  fixed, growing regression corpus (not just the fixture for the current fix) on every
324	  change; explicitly check *size* and *color* consistency across kinds, not just
325	  presence/position; test against the actual dev vault, not only synthetic fixtures;
326	  and treat a fully green test suite as necessary but not sufficient.
327	
328	## Current repository state
329	
330	The working tree has the full (broken) implementation uncommitted: `src/plugin/
331	decorate.ts`, `src/plugin/decorations.ts`, `styles.css`, the `main.ts` wiring, the e2e
332	spec, and updated OpenSpec artifacts for the `outline-decorations` change. Nothing has
333	been reverted — that decision (keep as a reference/starting point vs. discard) is left
334	for the next-iteration planning this document is meant to feed.
335

1	## Why
2	
3	Outline mode currently has no visual signal of its own: list items already show native
4	bullets, but heading and paragraph nodes — the whole point of the universal block-tree
5	model — render exactly like stock markdown. The first dev-vault verification round found
6	this is now the practical bottleneck: "with no bullets/indent chrome, outline mode is
7	hard to verify by eye in flat documents" (`docs/research/04-open-questions.md`), and flat
8	(paragraph-heavy) documents are precisely where the mapping algebra's paragraph-adjacency
9	rule needs to be seen to be trusted. Every structural op already works correctly; nobody
10	can see the tree they're editing.
11	
12	## What Changes
13	
14	- **Node chrome decorations**: a CM6 decoration layer, active only in outline mode (same
15	  per-keypress/per-render gating pattern as the keyboard grammar's `ModeSource`), that
16	  draws a leader marker at the start of every **paragraph** node's first line — the one
17	  node kind with no native visual leader at all. List items keep Obsidian's native
18	  bullet/number unchanged; headings keep their native `#`/typography; atoms (code, table,
19	  callout, quote, HTML, hr) already read as distinct blocks via their own chrome. Only
20	  paragraphs are invisible as tree nodes today, and flat (paragraph-only) documents are
21	  exactly where the dev-vault finding showed the gap.
22	- **Indentation guides by tree depth**: a vertical guide per ancestor level, computed from
23	  the node's position in the parsed tree — not from raw markdown indentation — so heading
24	  levels, list indentation, and paragraph adjacency (three different depth encodings) all
25	  read as one consistent visual hierarchy.
26	- Purely a rendering layer: `Decoration`/`ViewPlugin` only, via `registerEditorExtension`.
27	  Never edits the buffer, never changes `CachedMetadata`, no new file content, no new
28	  plugin-data state. Recomputes from the same `parse()` the grammar and commands already
29	  use, on the document changes CM6 reports (no separate polling/debounce mechanism).
30	- Outside outline mode, or on non-outline-mode files, decorations render nothing — the
31	  document looks byte-for-byte stock Obsidian, matching the existing "off-mode is stock"
32	  guarantee from `outline-mode` and `outline-keyboard-grammar`.
33	- Explicitly out of scope (later layers, per `docs/research/04-open-questions.md` Q4):
34	  fold chevrons and fold state/persistence, zoom/breadcrumbs, drag handles, node-selection
35	  highlight (needs the enforcement/selection layer first), any interactivity on the
36	  decorations themselves (click-to-select, click-to-fold) — this change is read-only
37	  chrome, not a new interaction surface.
38	
39	## Capabilities
40	
41	### New Capabilities
42	- `outline-decorations`: the CM6 decoration layer — which nodes get a marker, how tree
43	  depth maps to indentation guides across the three encodings (heading level, list
44	  indent, paragraph adjacency), the mode-gating and stock-when-off guarantee, and the
45	  non-mutating/no-reflow contract (decorations never alter document text, cursor
46	  position, or undo history).
47	
48	### Modified Capabilities
49	(none — this consumes `document-tree-mapping`'s `parse()` as-is and follows the existing
50	mode-gating pattern from `outline-mode` / `outline-keyboard-grammar` without changing
51	either spec's requirements)
52	
53	## Impact
54	
55	- New code: `src/plugin/decorations.ts` (pure depth/marker computation from an
56	  `OutlineDoc`, unit-testable without CM6) + a thin `ViewPlugin`/ `StateField` adapter
57	  registered in `main.ts` alongside `grammarExtension`, reusing the existing `ModeSource`
58	  gate.
59	- No changes to the mapping core (`src/parse.ts`, `src/ops.ts`, `src/model.ts`) — this is
60	  a read-only projection of the existing tree, not a new operation.
61	- No new dependencies — `@codemirror/view`'s `Decoration`/`ViewPlugin` APIs are already
62	  available (same package the keyboard grammar imports).
63	- Testing: unit tests for the pure marker/depth computation (mapping-core-style, no CM6);
64	  e2e coverage extends `e2e/specs/` with a visual/DOM-inspection suite (query rendered
65	  decoration elements rather than screenshots) per the existing wdio-obsidian-service
66	  harness.
67

1	> **⚠ Outcome: FAILED, 2026-07-13.** Every decision below was made, implemented, and
2	> re-verified in good faith at the time — but the shipped result was still broken in
3	> real vault use, and each "fix" in this document's revision history tended to introduce
4	> a new regression elsewhere. Read this design doc as a record of what was tried, not as
5	> a validated architecture. Full account, root causes, and carried-forward technical
6	> findings for the next attempt:
7	> [docs/research/06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md](../../../docs/research/06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md).
8	
9	## Context
10	
11	Outline mode's editing model (structural commands + keyboard grammar) is complete and
12	correct, but it is invisible: list items already render Obsidian's native bullet, so they
13	happen to look right, while heading and (especially) paragraph nodes render exactly like
14	stock markdown. The first dev-vault verification round hit this directly — flat,
15	paragraph-heavy documents give no visual signal that outline mode is even on, let alone
16	where node boundaries fall. This change adds a read-only CM6 decoration layer that makes
17	paragraph nodes visible as tree members: a leader marker and tree-depth indentation for
18	the one node kind with zero native chrome.
19	
20	The existing precedent to follow is `src/plugin/grammar.ts` + `src/plugin/keymap.ts`: a
21	pure module that computes a result from `(text, doc-position)` with no CM6 imports, and a
22	thin CM6 adapter that wires it into `registerEditorExtension`, gated by the same
23	`ModeSource`/`editorInfoField` pattern already used for the keyboard grammar and commands.
24	Decorations reuse this shape but produce a `DecorationSet` instead of a transaction.
25	
26	**Scope correction (2026-07-13, real-vault report):** the first implementation applied
27	indentation and a vertical guide line to *every* node kind, aiming for one visual
28	hierarchy across headings/lists/paragraphs. A real vault — not just synthetic e2e
29	fixtures — showed this broke native rendering outright: checkbox/list hanging-indent math
30	went visibly wrong (content landing to the left of where it should, i.e. "negative"
31	indentation), and the guide line was fixed at one screen position regardless of a line's
32	own depth, so at any real nesting it sat nowhere near the actual text. See D9. The scope
33	is now paragraphs only — the design below reflects that correction throughout, not the
34	original broader intent.
35	
36	## Goals / Non-Goals
37	
38	**Goals:**
39	- Paragraph nodes — the only kind with zero native leader or indentation — get a
40	  synthetic marker and tree-depth-based indentation, so a flat sequence of paragraphs
41	  visibly reads as outline nodes, and a paragraph nested via the paragraph-adjacency rule
42	  visibly nests.
43	- List items, headings, and atoms are byte-for-byte untouched by this layer — their
44	  existing native rendering (bullet/number with hanging indent, typography, block chrome)
45	  is left alone entirely, not overridden or augmented.
46	- Decorations are strictly read-only: no document mutation, no cursor movement, no undo
47	  entries, ever.
48	- Same mode-gating guarantee as the rest of the plugin: off outline mode (or file has
49	  never been toggled on), the editor is byte-for-byte stock Obsidian; toggling applies
50	  immediately, no reload.
51	- Mobile-safe (CM6 `Decoration`/CSS only, no Node/Electron APIs) and acceptable
52	  performance on typical single-note documents.
53	
54	**Non-Goals:**
55	- A cross-kind visual hierarchy (headings/lists/paragraphs sharing one indentation
56	  language) — this was the original goal and was dropped after D9; native list/heading
57	  indentation is not something this layer touches. A future change could pursue this
58	  properly (e.g. per-ancestor guide rendering that reads each kind's actual rendered
59	  indent rather than asserting one uniform unit), but it did not survive contact with
60	  real content and is not attempted again here.
61	- Fold chevrons or any fold state/persistence (separate later layer, per
62	  `docs/research/04-open-questions.md` Q4).
63	- Zoom/breadcrumbs, drag handles, node-selection highlight — all depend on layers not
64	  built yet (selection model, zoom).
65	- Interactivity on the decorations themselves (click-to-select, click-to-fold, hover
66	  affordances) — this change is passive chrome, not a new input surface.
67	- Configurable marker glyph/indent styling via a settings UI — v1 ships one fixed visual
68	  language; user CSS snippets remain the escape hatch, same as any other Obsidian styling.
69	- Reading (preview) view — untouched by construction; this plugin only registers CM6
70	  editor extensions, never a `MarkdownPostProcessor`.
71	
72	## Decisions
73	
74	### D1. Line decorations + a CSS custom property for depth, not per-depth classes
75	Each paragraph line gets a `Decoration.line` with an `attributes` style setting a
76	`--to-depth: N` CSS custom property; a single stylesheet rule uses `calc()` to derive
77	indentation from the variable. Alternative considered: a fixed set of discrete depth
78	classes (`.to-depth-0` … `.to-depth-10`). Rejected — nesting depth is unbounded, and a
79	capped class set either breaks at depth 11 or requires generating classes dynamically,
80	which a single `calc()` rule avoids entirely.
81	
82	### D2. The marker is a pure line-attribute decoration (no widget)
83	The paragraph leader marker is implemented as a `Decoration.line` attribute (a CSS class)
84	with the visible mark drawn by a CSS `::after` rule, not `Decoration.widget`. Alternative
85	considered: `Decoration.widget` DOM nodes for the marker (more flexible if it ever needs
86	interactivity). Rejected for v1 — widgets carry DOM-node lifecycle cost recomputed on
87	every viewport update and buy nothing while the marker is non-interactive (a Non-Goal).
88	Revisit if a later change makes markers clickable.
89	
90	### D3. Depth is derived by walking the parsed tree, not from source indentation/level
91	A node's depth is its distance from the document root in the `OutlineDoc` tree (root
92	children = depth 0), computed once per decoration pass by walking `doc.children`
93	recursively — the same walk shape as `startLine` in `grammar.ts`, counting every
94	ancestor's depth regardless of kind. Only paragraph facts are rendered (D9), but depth
95	still accounts for non-paragraph ancestors, so a paragraph nested three levels under
96	headings/lists still indents to depth 3 — the depth computation itself was never wrong;
97	only rendering it on non-paragraph lines was.
98	
99	### D4. Full-document recompute on every view update, no incremental diffing
100	The `ViewPlugin` recomputes the full `DecorationSet` from `parse(view.state.doc.toString())`
101	on every `ViewUpdate`, not gated to `docChanged` — the same "reparse the whole buffer"
102	budget the keyboard grammar and structural commands already spend per keystroke
103	(editor-core design.md: "fine on normal notes," incremental parsing deferred to whenever
104	an enforcement layer needs per-transaction trees anyway). Recomputing unconditionally,
105	rather than only on document changes, is what makes the mode toggle (D7) work: toggling
106	happens outside CM6 state (plugin data, not a transaction), so it needs *some* ViewUpdate
107	to surface it, and not gating on `docChanged` means that update doesn't need to carry a
108	document change to take effect. No new performance mechanism is introduced; if large-note
109	profiling ever shows a problem, CM6's viewport-restricted decoration APIs are the
110	documented escape hatch — not needed to start.
111	
112	### D5. Marker and indentation scope: paragraphs only, nothing else touched
113	Only paragraph nodes are decorated at all — not just the marker (the original scoping
114	decision), but now indentation too, and *no other kind receives any CSS from this layer,
115	full stop*. List items keep Obsidian's native bullet/number with its hanging-indent math
116	completely untouched; headings keep native `#`/typography; atoms
117	(code/table/callout/quote/html/hr) already read as distinct blocks via their own
118	borders/backgrounds. This was originally scoped to "just the marker," with indentation
119	and a guide line meant to apply everywhere for a unified hierarchy — D9 explains why that
120	broke and was walked back to this narrower, safer scope.
121	
122	### D6. Gating reuses the existing `ModeSource` contract
123	The decoration `ViewPlugin` takes the same `ModeSource` (`isOutline(path)`) the keyboard
124	grammar already depends on, checked via `editorInfoField` on every update — no new mode
125	plumbing. When the file isn't in outline mode, the plugin's `decorations` field is the
126	empty `DecorationSet`.
127	
128	### D7. Mode toggle forces a redraw via a self-tracked view registry + empty dispatch
129	Outline mode state lives in plugin data, entirely outside CM6's state — toggling it does
130	not by itself produce a `ViewUpdate`, so decorations would only pick up the change on the
131	next incidental edit or cursor move without an explicit poke. `decorations.ts` keeps a
132	module-level `Set<EditorView>` populated/cleared from the `ViewPlugin`'s own
133	constructor/`destroy()` — `EditorView` is handed to it by CM6 itself, the standard,
134	fully-public `ViewPlugin` contract, not a cast into anything private. `refreshDecorations()`
135	iterates that set and calls `view.dispatch({})` — an empty, content-free transaction,
136	itself documented public CM6 API — which produces a `ViewUpdate` (and thus an
137	`update()` call on every `ViewPlugin` instance) without touching the document. `main.ts`'s
138	`toggleMode` calls it right after persisting the toggle.
139	Alternatives considered and rejected: (a) the public `Editor.refresh()` bridge API
140	(`@public` since 0.11.11) — tried first as the more "official-looking" option, but
141	verified against a real Obsidian instance in the e2e suite to not produce a `ViewUpdate`
142	at all (it appears to be a measure/relayout hook, not a state transaction), so decorations
143	never appeared until an unrelated edit forced one; (b) reaching for the private
144	`(editor as any).cm` to dispatch directly — unnecessary once (a) was ruled out, and
145	exactly the private-API pattern this project avoids (research doc 03, feasibility notes).
146	The view registry needs no cast and is verified working end-to-end.
147	
148	### D8. CSS needs `!important` + a fixed `rem` unit, not `em`/`--list-indent`
149	Two things in `styles.css` turned out wrong on first implementation, both caught by
150	screenshotting the actual rendered output (not just asserting DOM attributes) during
151	verification:
152	- **Cascade**: a plain class selector on `.cm-line` (e.g. `.to-node-line { padding-left: … }`)
153	  was silently overridden — Obsidian's own line styling sets padding via an
154	  equal-or-higher-specificity rule later in the cascade, even on lines with none of
155	  Obsidian's own inline padding (so this wasn't just an inline-style conflict). Fixed with
156	  a more specific selector (`.cm-editor .cm-line.to-node-line`) plus `!important`. This
157	  reverses the original Risks-section aspiration of "no `!important`" — empirically, some
158	  properties on `.cm-line` need it to take effect at all.
159	- **Units**: the original rule read `var(--list-indent, 2em)` directly. CSS custom
160	  properties are substituted at the point of use, so the `em` in `--list-indent`'s
161	  definition resolved against each line's *own* font size — and heading lines render at a
162	  larger font than paragraph lines. Fixed by defining a plugin-local
163	  `:root { --to-indent-unit: 1.5rem; }` and using that instead — `rem` is root-relative,
164	  so it stays fixed regardless of the line's local font size.
165	
166	Both were invisible to a DOM-attribute-only check (the class and `--to-depth` custom
167	property were always set correctly) and only surfaced by rendering the fixtures and
168	looking — which motivated task 3.4's screenshot-based verification, but D9 shows even
169	that wasn't enough on its own (see below).
170	
171	### D9. Scope walked back to paragraph-only after a real-vault report — the guide line is gone
172	D8's fixes were verified against synthetic e2e fixtures (headings, plain lists, one
173	paragraph) and looked correct there — including in screenshots. Task 3.4 was marked done
174	on that basis. The user then tried the actual feature on their real vault (a daily journal
175	with checkbox task lists and deeply nested lists with mixed bullet/ordered markers) and
176	reported it "completely broken": indentation "sometimes negative," guide lines "all over
177	the place," including apparently when outline mode was off.
178	
179	Root causes, found by reproducing the exact content shapes:
180	- The `!important` `padding-left` override (D8) was applied to *every* decorated line,
181	  including list items. Obsidian's native list rendering pairs a negative `text-indent`
182	  (for the hanging bullet) with `padding-inline-start` — replacing only the padding half
183	  of that pair with our own value, while leaving the native `text-indent` untouched, could
184	  land content to the left of the bullet/checkbox instead of after it. This is what read
185	  as "negative" indentation.
186	- The vertical guide line (`::before`, a single stroke per line at a *fixed* screen
187	  position) was never repositioned per the line's own depth — it was drawn at roughly the
188	  same ~12px offset for every decorated line regardless of how much `padding-left` had
189	  shifted that line's actual content. At any real nesting depth the guide and the text it
190	  was meant to mark ended up nowhere near each other, reading as scattered, disconnected
191	  ticks. A correct version would need one stroke per ancestor level, positioned at that
192	  ancestor's own rendered indent — materially more work than a single always-present
193	  `::before`, and out of scope for this pass.
194	
195	Fix: **narrow the decorated surface to paragraph nodes only, and drop the guide line
196	entirely.** `decorate.ts` now tags every line of a paragraph node (not just its first)
197	with `isParagraph`; `decorations.ts` filters to `isParagraph` facts before building any
198	decoration, so list-item/heading/atom lines receive *zero* CSS from this layer — no class,
199	no custom property, nothing — regardless of their computed depth. Paragraphs have no
200	native indentation to conflict with, so `padding-left` there is purely additive and safe.
201	The guide line is removed from `styles.css`; only the padding-left indent and the marker
202	`::after` remain. Re-verified against the reported repro shapes (checkbox lists, mixed
203	bullet/ordered nesting) by screenshot: native rendering is now provably untouched (an e2e
204	regression test asserts every non-paragraph line's computed `padding-left` is
205	byte-identical between outline-mode-off and outline-mode-on).
206	
207	**Process note carried forward**: neither the DOM-attribute e2e assertions (3.2) nor the
208	first screenshot pass (3.4, synthetic fixtures) caught this — it took a real vault with
209	real content shapes (checkboxes, mixed list markers) to surface it. `docs/research/
210	04-open-questions.md` already carries a finding about DOM-attribute checks being
211	insufficient for CSS; this adds that synthetic fixtures aren't a full substitute for
212	testing against real vault content either, at least for anything touching shared
213	rendering surfaces like `.cm-line`.
214	
215	## Risks / Trade-offs
216	
217	- **Full reparse per transaction on very large notes** → could add latency on pathological
218	  documents. Mitigation: same budget already accepted for grammar/commands; not a new
219	  risk this change introduces, and CM6 viewport-scoped decorations are a known escape
220	  hatch if profiling ever calls for it.
221	- **No cross-kind visual hierarchy** → a paragraph nested under a list item indents by our
222	  fixed unit, while its list-item siblings indent by Obsidian's own (different, per-level)
223	  native amount — the two won't necessarily line up pixel-for-pixel at "the same" nominal
224	  tree depth. Accepted per D9: correctness and not breaking native rendering wins over a
225	  unified hierarchy for v1; a real fix needs per-ancestor guide rendering, not attempted
226	  here.
227	- **CSS guide alignment across themes/fonts** → a fixed `rem` unit (D8) is root-relative,
228	  not tied to any one element's font size, so this is largely mitigated for the
229	  (now paragraph-only) indentation; unusual theme-level root font-size changes or RTL text
230	  are still untested — verify visually in the dev-vault pass across at least the default
231	  light/dark themes.
232	- **Community CSS snippets/themes overriding our styling** → narrowly scoped, `to-`
233	  prefixed class names, but D8 found `!important` genuinely necessary for the padding
234	  rule to take effect at all against Obsidian's own line styling. A community snippet
235	  targeting the same property with its own `!important` could still win; document as a
236	  known limitation rather than trying to out-specificity every possible snippet. Scoping
237	  to paragraph-only (D9) also shrinks the blast radius of any such conflict considerably.
238	- **Visual redundancy if obsidian-outliner/obsidian-zoom are also enabled** → already
239	  covered by the existing one-time coexistence warning Notice; no additional code-level
240	  guard needed.
241	
242	## Migration Plan
243	
244	Purely additive UI layer — no data model, no file content, no plugin-data schema changes.
245	Ships as a normal minor change: build, unit tests for the pure depth/marker computation,
246	e2e DOM-inspection coverage, manual dev-vault visual pass. No rollback concerns beyond
247	disabling the plugin, which already restores stock rendering.
248	
249	## Open Questions
250	
251	- A real cross-kind visual hierarchy (D9's dropped Non-Goal) — worth revisiting as its own
252	  scoped change once there's a clearer plan for per-ancestor guide rendering that reads
253	  each kind's actual native indent rather than asserting a uniform unit over it.
254	- Marker/guide interactivity (click-to-select, click-to-fold) is explicitly deferred; the
255	  node-selection and fold layers will need to decide whether they attach behavior to these
256	  same decorations or introduce their own.

2	
3	### Requirement: Decorations are scoped to outline mode
4	The decoration layer SHALL be registered as a CodeMirror extension via
5	`registerEditorExtension` and SHALL render node chrome only when the editor's file has
6	outline mode enabled (resolved through the same mode source the keyboard grammar uses).
7	Outside outline mode, or in the reading/preview view, the document SHALL render exactly as
8	stock Obsidian, with no markers or other decorations present.
9	
10	#### Scenario: No chrome off-mode
11	- **WHEN** a note without outline mode enabled is open in the editor
12	- **THEN** no paragraph indentation or node markers are rendered; the DOM matches stock
13	  Obsidian live preview
14	
15	#### Scenario: Toggle applies immediately
16	- **WHEN** outline mode is toggled on for the open note
17	- **THEN** markers and paragraph indentation appear on the next editor render, with no
18	  reload required
19	
20	### Requirement: Decoration is scoped to paragraph nodes only
21	Only paragraph nodes SHALL receive indentation or marker decoration from this layer.
22	List-item, heading, and atom lines SHALL receive no decoration at all from this layer —
23	no CSS class, no custom property, nothing — regardless of their computed tree depth, so
24	their existing native rendering (bullet/number with hanging indent, heading style, block
25	chrome) is left completely unaffected. This is a hard requirement, not a preference:
26	overriding native list/heading line styling was tried and found to corrupt native
27	hanging-indent rendering (checkbox/bullet layout) on real content — see design.md D9.
28	
29	#### Scenario: Native list and heading rendering is untouched
30	- **WHEN** a note containing headings, checkbox list items, and nested lists is toggled
31	  into outline mode
32	- **THEN** every non-paragraph line's computed indentation is byte-identical to its
33	  outline-mode-off rendering, and no marker is added to any of them
34	
35	### Requirement: Paragraph indentation reflects tree depth
36	Every line of a paragraph node SHALL be indented by an amount proportional to the node's
37	distance from the document root in the parsed tree, not the raw markdown indentation
38	(paragraphs have none natively). A paragraph nested via the paragraph-adjacency rule
39	SHALL indent by one increment relative to its parent paragraph.
40	
41	#### Scenario: Paragraph-adjacency depth is visible
42	- **WHEN** a paragraph is indented under a previous-sibling paragraph (encoded as a list
43	  item per the mapping algebra)
44	- **THEN** its indentation is one increment deeper than its parent paragraph's, matching
45	  its tree position
46	
47	#### Scenario: Continuation lines indent with their node
48	- **WHEN** a paragraph node spans multiple lines
49	- **THEN** every line of that node — not just the first — carries the same indentation
50	
51	#### Scenario: Gap lines carry no decoration
52	- **WHEN** a paragraph node is followed by its `trailingGap` blank lines
53	- **THEN** the decoration ends at the node's own last line; the blank gap lines that
54	  follow render with no decoration
55	
56	### Requirement: Paragraph nodes render a leader marker
57	Every paragraph node's first line SHALL render a synthetic leader marker.
58	
59	#### Scenario: Flat paragraph sequence is visible as nodes
60	- **WHEN** a document is a flat sequence of top-level paragraphs with no lists or headings
61	- **THEN** each paragraph's first line renders a leader marker, distinguishing it as a
62	  node even though the document has no native list or heading chrome
63	
64	### Requirement: Decorations never mutate document state
65	The decoration layer SHALL be a pure rendering projection: it SHALL NOT dispatch any
66	document-changing transaction, move the cursor/selection, or create undo history entries,
67	regardless of how often it recomputes.
68	
69	#### Scenario: Rendering produces no transaction
70	- **WHEN** the decoration layer recomputes after a document change
71	- **THEN** no new transaction is dispatched beyond the one that triggered the recompute,
72	  and the document text, cursor position, and undo stack are exactly as they were left by
73	  that triggering change

7	## Q1. Architecture path ✅ DECIDED: editor-centric (Option A/C)
8	
9	CM6 extensions in the standard markdown view + our own side panes. 100% public API.
10	See [03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md](03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md).

12	## Q2. Scope of "outline mode" ✅ DECIDED: universal isomorphic outline view
13	
14	Neither per-file opt-in nor vault-wide enforcement. The owner's framing (near-verbatim):
15	
16	> The outliner experience is all about the **editor UI, not the content under it**. Any "flat"
17	> markdown note actually has structure: a hierarchy implied by headings of different levels,
18	> each paragraph is a separate node, lists are natural outliner subtrees. Any existing markdown
19	> note can be transformed into an outline by mapping that hierarchical structure onto the tree,
20	> providing the same editing experience as for a nested bullet-list outline. A UI toggle turns
21	> any note into an outline (no change for list-only content), and back. **This has to be an
22	> isomorphic transformation.**
23	
24	Implications:
25	
26	- The document model is the **full markdown block tree**: heading nodes (level = depth
27	  anchor), block nodes (paragraph, code fence, quote, table, …), and list-item nodes —
28	  not just lists. Obsidian's `CachedMetadata` (`sections` + `headings` + `listItems`) already
29	  parses exactly this tree — see the feasibility addendum.
30	- The toggle is a **view/UI mode change, never a content rewrite**. For a pure-list note the
31	  outline view is visually identical to today's list editing.
32	- **Isomorphism = lossless round-trip**: md → tree → md must be identity; every structural
33	  operation in outline mode must produce a tree that maps back to valid, natural markdown.
34	- "Both without compromises": flat-markdown notes keep full outliner features *through the
35	  mapping*, instead of losing them (obsidian-outliner's model) or being forced into bullets
36	  (Logseq's model).
37	
38	### Q2 follow-ups (the mapping algebra) ✅ DECIDED (2026-07-12, second alignment)
39	
40	**The unifying principle** (governs all structural ops):
41	
42	> Every structural op either writes the **minimal markdown encoding of the new tree**, or —
43	> when no encoding exists — is **rejected with gentle feedback**. Never hidden state, never
44	> lossy conversion.
45	
46	**The two-regime algebra** (third alignment refinement):
47	
48	```
49	HEADINGS         Tab/S-Tab = level ± 1 (org-mode promote/demote), whole
50	                 subtree shifts; the tree re-derives from levels; reject
51	                 only at the h1/h6 bounds.
52	EVERYTHING ELSE  Tab/S-Tab = reparent (child-of-previous-sibling /
53	                 brother→uncle); encoding recomputed from the new context.
54	ALWAYS           minimal encoding or reject; no hidden state.
55	```
56	
57	1. **Paragraph under paragraph** ✅ *provisional*: a list following a paragraph is that
58	   paragraph's **children** in outline mode. So indenting paragraph B under paragraph A turns
59	   B into a list item after A; A stays an intact paragraph. Top-level paragraphs are never
60	   auto-converted — a flat document just becomes a long flat list of nodes when toggled.
61	   *Marked provisional*: revisit after the first prototype; may become configurable.
62	   *Alternatives considered and rejected*: (a) lists attach only to headings, never
63	   paragraphs — makes paragraph-with-children inexpressible, killing the indent-under-
64	   paragraph op entirely; (b) sentinel syntax (e.g. a paragraph ending in `:` claims the
65	   following list) — magic content-sniffing, fails isomorphism. Adjacency-as-parenthood is
66	   the only lossless way to give paragraphs children; the heading/paragraph asymmetry is
67	   markdown's (headings *scope* what follows; paragraphs merely *precede* it).
68	2. **Heading nodes** ✅: Tab/Shift+Tab = **level ± 1** (org-mode promote/demote semantics),
69	   the whole subtree's headings shift with it (marker-only edits; `[[note#Heading]]` anchors
70	   are text-based and survive). Rejected only at the bounds (no h0, no h7). **Skipped levels**
71	   (h1 → h3) are preserved; tree depth = tree position, not raw level; outdenting a
72	   skip-leveled heading first normalizes the level (### → ## under an h1: level changes,
73	   hierarchy doesn't), then the next outdent changes hierarchy — and symmetrically, Tab on a
74	   heading may create a skip (a "styling-only" edit where the node's tree position is
75	   unchanged and only the visible level marker deepens). Accepted consequence, same as org.
76	3. **Context-determined encoding on reparent** ✅ *provisional*: a reparented node's encoding
77	   is a pure function of its new surroundings — it takes the type of its nearest preceding
78	   sibling under the new parent (fallback: following sibling; no siblings: paragraph under a
79	   heading/root, list item under anything else). Consequences: paragraph → indent → outdent
80	   round-trips back to a paragraph (new sibling is a paragraph), while outdenting inside a
81	   100%-nested-list document keeps everything list items (all neighbors are list items) — no
82	   flattening, and no hidden per-node memory of original types.
83	4. **Mixed containment** ✅: a list item can never contain a heading — under the two-regime
84	   algebra no op can even attempt it (headings move by level, not by reparenting), so this
85	   holds by construction rather than by rejection.
86	5. **Leaf-only blocks** ✅: code fences, tables, callouts, quotes are movable/indentable
87	   **atoms**; internal content is not outline-structured.
88	6. **Toggle persistence** ✅: remembered per note in the **plugin data store** (consistent
89	   with Q3 — files stay clean; frontmatter would pollute content for a pure UI mode).
90	
91	See [05-org-mode-comparison.md](05-org-mode-comparison.md) for where this algebra aligns
92	with and diverges from org-mode, the closest living reference system.
93	
94	### Verdicts from the mapping-core implementation (2026-07-12, `mapping-core` change)
95	
96	Both provisional rules **held up** under property testing (byte-identity round-trip,
97	op-closure, inverse laws — thousands of generated cases). Findings to carry forward:
98	
99	- **Attachment rule: KEEP.** Confirmed consequence: "list item as the sibling directly
100	  after a paragraph" is unrepresentable — the tree generator itself had to fold such
101	  shapes into children, which is the rule working as designed. Scope refinement
102	  discovered: in v1 the rule applies at *section level* (root/heading children); inside
103	  a list item's children, a paragraph and a following list parse as siblings under the
104	  item. Revisit whether nested paragraphs should also capture lists.
105	- **Context-determined encoding: KEEP.** Delivered exactly the promised laws: paragraph
106	  indent∘outdent restores the document byte-identically, and pure-list documents never
107	  flatten. The donor scan considers only paragraph/list-item siblings (headings/atoms
108	  are skipped) — heading nodes are never produced by re-encoding.
109	- **New rejection discovered — outdent out of a heading section.** Heading scope is
110	  positional in markdown: content placed "after the section" is still *in* the section,
111	  so brother→uncle for a direct child of a heading has no encoding → rejected
112	  (`not-expressible-under-target`). UX implication for the CM6 layer: outdent at
113	  section level needs affordance messaging (or a future "split section" op).
114	- **Reordering across the heading/content divide is rejected**, and heading swaps
115	  require equal levels — same positional-encoding reason.
116	- **Minimal-edit tradeoff**: indenting a paragraph into an existing child list keeps
117	  the old separator blank line with the untouched sibling (a loose list — same tree).
118	  Cosmetic; a "tidy gaps" pass could be a later opt-in.
119	
120	### Dev-vault findings (2026-07-13, first human verification round)
121	
122	- **Indentation unit: RESOLVED (was an open design question).** Obsidian indents lists
123	  with tabs by default; synthesizing space-based indentation silently double-outdented
124	  tab lists (dedent overshoot swallowed whole tabs). Fix: a reparented node **adopts the
125	  destination's indentation string verbatim** — an existing sibling item's, else the
126	  parent's plus one unit inferred from the document (default two spaces). Plugin-side
127	  config passthrough of Obsidian's own indent settings remains a possible refinement.
128	- **Cross-parent heading moves**: moveUp/Down is same-parent sibling swap in v1, so a
129	  heading that is an only child rejects with no-sibling — surprising in practice.
130	  A "move into adjacent section" op is expressible (the heading must land AFTER the
131	  destination section's direct content, mirroring the indent op's before-first-subheading
132	  rule — this also keeps the destination's paragraphs out of the moved subtree).
133	  Candidate for the next structural change.
134	- **Visual layer is now the testability bottleneck**: with no bullets/indent chrome,
135	  outline mode is hard to verify by eye in flat documents. Decorations change moves up
136	  in priority.
137	
138	### Verdict from the outline-decorations implementation (2026-07-13): FAILED
139	
140	Three full redesign cycles, 198 unit tests, 33 e2e tests (all green), and multiple
141	screenshot-verification rounds later, the feature was still visibly broken in real vault
142	use — marker size scaling with heading font-size, inconsistent indentation, wrong
143	continuation-line indentation, markers overlapping native bullets, misplaced guide
144	lines. The CSS-override strategy (own `padding-left`/`text-indent`/`margin-left` via
145	`!important`) kept producing new regressions each time a prior one was patched, and the
146	testing approach (DOM-attribute assertions, narrow synthetic fixtures, tests updated to
147	match whatever the implementation currently did) gave false confidence at every stage.
148	Full account, root causes, and carried-forward technical findings:
149	[06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md](06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md). The
150	"visual layer is the testability bottleneck" problem above is **still open** — this
151	attempt did not resolve it.
152	
153	## Q3. Node identity & metadata storage ✅ DECIDED
154	
155	Native `^block-id` **on demand** (only when a node is actually referenced); collapse state in
156	the **plugin data store**; files stay clean. Multiline nodes as standard markdown continuation
157	lines (to be specced in detail).
158	
159	## Q4. MVP cut ✅ DECIDED: small, solid core
160	
161	v1 = the enforced core editor (grammar + node selection + structure invariants) built on an
162	architecture that keeps every later layer open (fold persistence, zoom, structured backlinks,
163	refs/mirrors, drag-and-drop, search). Layers land one at a time on top of the stable core.
164	
165	**Refinement (second alignment)**: the core implements the **universal block-tree model from
166	day 1** — headings + paragraphs + lists as nodes, per Q2. The MVP is smaller in *features*
167	(minimal chrome, no zoom/backlinks/DnD yet), not in *model*. Retrofitting the tree model onto
168	a list-only core later would risk a rewrite.
169	
170	## Q5. Relationship to existing plugins ✅ DECIDED: build fresh
171	
172	Build fresh — Q2's universal mapping needs a tree-model core that the list-only engines don't
173	have. Use obsidian-outliner / zoom / pro-outliner as proof-of-primitive references; borrow
174	patterns with MIT attribution where genuinely useful. Coexistence: detect obsidian-outliner /
175	obsidian-zoom at load and show a one-time warning notice.
176	
177	## Q6. Interop & degradation guarantees ✅ CONFIRMED as hard requirements
178	
179	Files remain plain markdown, fully usable with the plugin disabled/uninstalled; no gratuitous
180	rewriting of untouched lines (whitespace, bullets, mtimes); other-tool edits (sync, mobile
181	without plugin, scripts) never corrupt anything on re-open. To be written into the project
182	spec as invariants.
183	
184	## Q7. Mobile support ✅ DECIDED: mobile-safe, desktop-tested
185	
186	Hard rules from day 1: no Node/Electron APIs, `isMobile`-aware, CM6/DOM only (the editor-
187	centric architecture already guarantees this). Test/polish desktop only for v1.0; declare
188	mobile support in a v1.x milestone after real-device testing.
189	
190	## Q8. Vim mode ✅ DECIDED: out of scope for v1
191	
192	The only known path is the undocumented `window.CodeMirrorAdapter.Vim` hack, which fails the
193	perfect-scorecard bar. Document as a known limitation.
194	
195	## Q9. Node content richness ✅ DECIDED: plain content in v1
196	
197	Checkboxes/tasks are plain content in v1 — preserved perfectly (isomorphism guarantees it),
198	no special node state or UX. First-class task states are a clean later layer.
199	
200	## Q10. Backlinks pane placement ❓ (post-v1)
201	
202	Our structured backlinks as: a sidebar pane (like core backlinks), an in-document footer
203	section (like influx/Logseq), or both? Replace-core-pane is off the table (private APIs).
204	
205	## Q11. Undo/redo scope ✅ DECIDED: CM6-native undo for v1
206	
207	With deliberate transaction grouping (`userEvent` annotations) so one structural op = one undo
208	step. View-state restoration (fold/zoom/focus) is a later enhancement layer.
209	
210	## Q12. Name & positioning ✅ DECIDED: working name stays; final name at submission
211	
212	"True Outliner" as working name; decide the final name at directory-submission time.
213	Differentiator statement: *any note is an outline — enforced structure, node selection,
214	isomorphic markdown mapping — one coherent plugin*.
215	
216	## Q13. Parser: custom vs. remark/mdast/micromark ✅ DECIDED (2026-07-13): keep custom, revisit trigger defined
217	
218	Re-examined design.md D1 against 2026 research before committing to the hand-rolled parser
219	long-term. **Verdict: keep it.** Findings:
220	
221	- Obsidian's internal parser is an undocumented black box; the "remark-parse 8" claim
222	  circulating in the forums is unverified speculation, not a confirmed fact — there's nothing
223	  to "align with" even if we wanted to.
224	- `mdast-util-to-markdown` is documented as **not** round-trip-safe (confirmed upstream issue:
225	  parse→stringify can change AST structure on re-parse). Adopting it would directly break the
226	  byte-identity goal (design.md D1/D2) — the exact failure mode D1 already rejected it for.
227	- The OFM-extension remark plugin ecosystem (wikilinks, callouts, embeds, block-refs) is
228	  fragmented and often stale/single-maintainer; we'd hand-write most of it anyway — and we
229	  don't need it, since our model keeps all OFM constructs as opaque content inside block
230	  nodes (never inline-parsed).
231	- **Revisit trigger**: if corpus/property testing surfaces real CommonMark-dialect bugs our
232	  segmenter is structurally bad at (lazy continuation, nested list/blockquote edge cases),
233	  the upgrade path is **micromark's core tokenizer only** (not mdast/remark) as a
234	  boundary/offset oracle feeding our existing `OutlineNode`/encode/ops unchanged — this
235	  "tokenize with micromark, keep your own tree" pattern is exactly how `mdast-util-from-markdown`
236	  itself is built, so it's proven architecture, just not something to adopt preemptively.
237
