1	# The Outliner Landscape
2	
3	## What an outliner is (and is not)
4	
5	An [outliner](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Outliner) is a text editor whose document model is a
6	**tree of nodes**, not a sequence of lines. Content is organized by hierarchical relationship
7	rather than linear flow. The classic taxonomy:
8	
9	- **One-pane (intrinsic)** outliners — structure and content in one view; sections collapse and
10	  expand in place. This is the model of Workflowy, Roam, Logseq, Tana, and this project.
11	- **Two-pane (extrinsic)** outliners — a structure tree in one pane, node content in another
12	  (classic examples: OmniOutliner-style tools, many "TOC sidebar" designs).
13	
14	**Not the same thing:** Obsidian's "Outline" core plugin (heading TOC sidebar) is a *view of*
15	a linear document. We are concerned with the document *being* an outline and the *editing
16	experience* of it — the opposite end of the spectrum.
17	
18	## The defining behaviors of a "true outliner"
19	
20	This is the checklist that separates a real outliner from "a text editor that's good at lists."
21	Compiled from Workflowy/Roam/Logseq/Tana/Dynalist behavior and from long-standing community
22	requests (e.g. the Obsidian forum's
23	["Outliner mode (similar to Logseq, Roam, Dynalist)"](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/outliner-mode-similar-to-logseq-roam-dynalist/13769)
24	thread, where users describe list editing in Obsidian as *"fragile"* — "requires the cursor to
25	be in just the right place and copying, moving things around often breaks things").
26	
27	### 1. Node identity and atomicity
28	
29	Every visible bullet is a **node** (block) with an identity. Operations act on nodes, never on
30	raw text lines. A node carries its subtree with it through every operation.
31	
32	### 2. Structure is enforced, not suggested
33	
34	The document **cannot be malformed**. There is no keystroke, selection, paste, or deletion that
35	produces broken indentation, orphaned children, or "text floating next to the outline."
36	This is the single biggest gap in every existing Obsidian plugin — with flat markdown
37	underneath, a sloppy select-and-delete shreds the tree.
38	
39	### 3. The editing grammar
40	
41	The near-universal key grammar across Workflowy/Roam/Logseq/Tana:
42	
43	| Input | Behavior |
44	| --- | --- |
45	| `Enter` | New sibling below (new first child if node has visible children — app-dependent); splits the node if cursor is mid-text |
46	| `Shift+Enter` | Soft line break *inside* the node (multiline node) |
47	| `Tab` / `Shift+Tab` | Indent / outdent the node (with subtree), **regardless of cursor position in the line**; no-op (not text-tab) when structurally impossible |
48	| `Backspace` at start of node | Merge into previous node / delete empty node — never leaves orphans; children handled by promotion or merge rules |
49	| `Enter` on empty indented node | Outdent (progressive escape), rather than creating empty siblings |
50	| Move up/down (`Alt/Cmd+Shift+↑/↓`) | Swap node (with subtree) with sibling; crossing a parent boundary re-parents predictably |
51	| `Cmd+↑/↓` or chevron | Collapse / expand |
52	
53	### 4. The selection model (the acid test)
54	
55	- Text selection *within* a node behaves like a normal editor.
56	- The moment a selection crosses a node boundary, it **escalates to node selection**: whole
57	  nodes (with their subtrees) are selected, visually as blocks — never a ragged half-line span.
58	- `Esc` (or repeated `Cmd+A`) escalates: text → node → node+subtree → all siblings → whole outline.
59	- Cut/copy of a node selection carries structure; paste re-anchors the subtree at the target
60	  with indentation normalized to the destination depth.
61	- Delete of a node selection removes whole nodes; the tree is valid afterwards, always.
62	
63	This is where "flat text + cursor tricks" visibly falls apart, and it is the user's primary
64	stated pain with obsidian-outliner.
65	
66	### 5. Fold / collapse
67	
68	- Any node with children collapses; indicator on the bullet (and child count on hover, à la Workflowy).
69	- Collapsed state **persists** across sessions (where it's stored is a key design decision — see lessons below).
70	- Navigation and search interact sanely with folds (folded matches auto-reveal).
71	
72	### 6. Zoom (hoisting)
73	
74	- Any node can become the temporary root of the view ("zoom in" / "hoist" / Logseq "focus").
75	- Breadcrumbs show the ancestor path; clicking a crumb zooms to it.
76	- Zoom is a *view* state, not a document mutation. (Workflowy takes this to the extreme: the
77	  entire product is one infinite outline you're always zoomed into some part of.)
78	
79	### 7. Drag and drop
80	
81	Grab the bullet handle, drag the node (with subtree); drop indicators show the target position
82	*and depth*. Dropping can re-parent.
83	
84	### 8. References, mirrors, and bi-directional outlining
85	
86	The "networked thought" layer built on top of node identity:
87	
88	- **Block references** — link to a node from elsewhere; `((...))` in Roam/Logseq,
89	  `[[note#^block-id]]` natively in Obsidian.
90	- **Block embeds / mirrors** — a node rendered (or even *edited*) in another location;
91	  Roam/Workflowy mirrors, Tana "search nodes," Logseq embeds.
92	- **Structured backlinks** ("bi-directional outlining") — the backlinks view for a page/node
93	  shows each referencing *block in its tree context*: ancestor breadcrumb above, children below,
94	  editable in place in the best implementations (Roam, Logseq). This is dramatically more useful
95	  than Obsidian's flat line-match backlinks pane, and it's a stated goal of this project.
96	  [Orca Note](https://orca-studio.com/orcanote/) markets itself precisely on being an
97	  "advanced fine-grained bi-directional outliner."
98	
99	### 9. The rest of the table stakes
100	
101	- **Undo/redo** restores structure *and* focus/selection/fold state coherently.
102	- **Performance**: every keystroke instant on multi-thousand-node documents.
103	- **Keyboard-first completeness** — every mouse affordance has a keybinding.
104	- **Rich inline content** — inline markdown (bold/links/tags/checkboxes) inside nodes;
105	  multiline nodes; per-node "document-like" content (code blocks, quotes) as an advanced case.
106	- **Search/filter** that shows matches with their ancestor context.
107	
108	## The reference apps
109	
110	### Workflowy — the gold standard for interaction
111	
112	One infinite zoomable outline; minimal formatting; nearly flawless keyboard and selection
113	mechanics; mirrors added later. Its lesson: **interaction polish beats feature count**. Every
114	outliner is still judged against Workflowy's editing feel.
115	
116	### Roam Research — refined block grammar
117	
118	Popularized block references, block embeds, daily notes, and structured backlinks. Its outliner
119	mechanics (selection escalation, block manipulation shortcuts) remain among the tightest.
120	Cloud-only, proprietary, and a fading force — but the interaction patterns are canon.
121	
122	### Logseq — the existence proof *and* the cautionary tale
123	
124	Logseq proved a true outliner can sit on top of **plain markdown files** — and paid dearly for
125	the details:
126	
127	- **In-file metadata pollution**: `id:: 6a1f...` properties injected into the markdown when a
128	  block is referenced, `collapsed:: true` written on fold, `:LOGBOOK:` drawers, forced `-`
129	  bullet on every line. Files round-trip poorly with other tools
130	  ([community property-syntax discussion](https://discuss.logseq.com/t/an-idea-for-a-more-standard-markdown-property-syntax/20073));
131	  an entire Obsidian plugin ([Logseq Formater](https://community.obsidian.md/plugins/logseq-formater))
132	  exists just to clean Logseq artifacts out of shared vaults.
133	- **Whitespace/indentation instability** across versions (e.g.
134	  [markdown export mangling indentation](https://github.com/logseq/logseq/issues/9805)).
135	- **The rewrite**: years spent on the "DB version," culminating in the April 2026
136	  [split into two products](https://logseq.io/page/b2ad9ce1-9cb7-4436-8083-54cb4516d324/df4dc09d-0a12-4c87-904e-22a9bf4c350a):
137	  **Logseq OG** (file-based, maintenance-only: security + Electron updates) and **Logseq DB**
138	  (database-first, the future, currently beta). I.e., the flagship markdown outliner team
139	  concluded that markdown-as-database, *as they had built it*, was a dead end.
140	
141	**Lessons for us**: (a) keep the markdown clean — metadata belongs out-of-band or in native
142	Obsidian syntax; (b) don't rewrite user files gratuitously; (c) scope discipline — Logseq
143	drowned trying to be an outliner *and* a database *and* a task manager *and* a sync service.
144	
145	### outl — the modern minimal answer
146	
147	[outl](https://outl.app/) (open source, Rust, 2025–26): "plain markdown is the source of truth",
148	**"the CRDT lives in a sidecar, not in your files"** — sync IDs live in a `.outl` sidecar so the
149	`.md` stays clean ("delete outl tomorrow, your notes still read the same in any text editor").
150	Wiki-links/backlinks out of the box, daily journal, vim-style navigation, P2P sync
151	(Kleppmann-style tree CRDT), MCP server. Its lesson: **the sidecar pattern** reconciles stable
152	node identity with clean markdown. Young project; multi-surface (TUI/desktop/iOS).
153	
154	### Tana — structure on top of the outline
155	
156	Every node can carry a **supertag** (a type with fields), turning the outline into a queryable
157	graph. In March 2026 Tana [split into two products](https://outliner.tana.inc/articles/tana-current-march-2026):
158	**Tana Outliner** continues for outline devotees while "new Tana" pivots to team/AI/meetings.
159	Lesson: the outliner core has a loyal audience distinct from the "AI workspace" market; also,
160	supertags show where an outline+metadata model can go later (out of scope for our MVP).
161	
162	### Orca Note — fine-grained bi-directional outlining
163	
164	Local-first, block-based, "advanced fine-grained bi-directional outliner," super tags, block-level
165	backlinks, plugin API + CLI, free tier limited to 1,000 blocks. Closed-source, small team.
166	Relevant mostly as a benchmark for the backlinks-with-structure experience.
167	
168	### Dynalist (honorable mention)
169	
170	Workflowy competitor by the makers of Obsidian; effectively frozen since Obsidian took off.
171	Its polish level and its neglect are both instructive: Obsidian's makers know outliners
172	intimately — and chose a free-form editor for Obsidian. The gap is real and deliberate;
173	filling it is plugin territory.
174	
175	## The architectural axis every app sits on
176	
177	| Source of truth | Apps | Consequence |
178	| --- | --- | --- |
179	| **Markdown files** | Logseq OG, outl, (Obsidian itself) | Interop, longevity, user trust; hard: node identity, metadata, performance at scale |
180	| **Database** | Roam, Tana, Orca, Logseq DB | Easy node identity/refs/queries; hard: lock-in, export fidelity, user trust |
181	
182	An Obsidian plugin doesn't get to choose: **markdown files in the vault are the source of
183	truth**, non-negotiable. That makes Logseq's mistakes and outl's sidecar idea our most relevant
184	prior art, and it frames the central design problem: *how much outliner metadata (node IDs,
185	collapse state) lives where — in the file (native syntax only?), in plugin data, or in a sidecar?*
186	
187	## The TUI prototype (`tui-outliner-multiline`)
188	
189	The author's SpecKit-based TUI experiment. What transfers to this project:
190	
191	- **Behavior inventories worth mining**: complete acceptance-scenario catalogs for navigation,
192	  create/edit, structural ops (incl. cross-parent move semantics), fold, zoom
193	  (focus-after-zoom rules, breadcrumb rules, "left on top node zooms up one level"), and an
194	  exceptionally careful **undo/redo focus-restoration spec** (undo restores *where focus was
195	  before the operation*; delete-undo focuses the restored node; focus must never land on an
196	  invisible root).
197	- **Engineering patterns that held up**: pure tree-operation functions separated from UI;
198	  immutable tree updates enabling snapshot undo; precomputed visible-node lists; performance
199	  budgets validated by benchmark tests (sub-ms navigation at 500+ nodes).
200	- **Multiline nodes** as first-class (`Shift+Enter`), and read-only outline mode.
201	
202	What does *not* transfer (TUI bias):
203	
204	- The **modal nav/edit split** (vim-style `i`/`a`/`o`/`Esc`, single-focused-node model).
205	  Obsidian is direct-manipulation: cursor lives in text, editing is always on. The outliner
206	  grammar must be modeless (with a *transient* node-selection state, not a mode).
207	- JSON file format, single-file model, terminal key constraints, manual scroll management.
208

1	# Obsidian Plugin Landscape
2	
3	What exists today, how each plugin works internally, and where the gaps are.
4	(Internals verified against source via DeepWiki, July 2026.)
5	
6	## Editing-side plugins
7	
8	### [obsidian-outliner](https://github.com/vslinko/obsidian-outliner) (vslinko)
9	
10	The incumbent, and the baseline to improve upon.
11	
12	**How it works.** A features/services architecture on top of official APIs:
13	`registerEditorExtension` with CodeMirror 6 keymaps at `Prec.highest` (Enter, Tab), an
14	`EditorState.transactionExtender` that keeps the cursor inside list content and out of folded
15	regions ("stick cursor"), CM6 extensions for drag-and-drop and vertical indent lines, and its
16	own markdown list parser + operation engine (`Parser`, `OperationPerformer`) working through a
17	`MyEditor` wrapper. Mostly clean API usage; the exceptions are `(editor as any).cm` to reach the
18	CM6 `EditorView` (ubiquitous community pattern, not in public typings) and
19	`window.CodeMirrorAdapter.Vim` for vim `o`/`O` support.
20	
21	**What it delivers**: move-with-children, indent/outdent, folding shortcuts, smarter
22	Enter/Tab, Ctrl+A escalation (item → whole list), drag-and-drop, indent guides, list styling.
23	
24	**Where it falls short of a true outliner** (the motivating gaps for this project):
25	
26	- **No structure enforcement.** It's "a flat text with cursor tricks bolted on top" — nothing
27	  stops a newline from starting flat paragraph text next to the outline; nothing validates the
28	  tree.
29	- **No node-level selection model.** Selections are raw text spans; the README itself lists
30	  *"manipulation with multiple lines"* as not supported. A careless select-and-delete still
31	  shreds structure and leaves broken indentation.
32	- No zoom (delegated to the companion plugin), no node identity, no references/mirrors,
33	  no structured backlinks — it is purely a list-editing enhancer.
34	- 95 open issues; maintained but conservative in scope (v4.10.2, June 2026).
35	
36	### [obsidian-zoom](https://github.com/vslinko/obsidian-zoom) (vslinko)
37	
38	Zoom into a list subtree. **How it works**: a CM6 `StateField` dispatching effects that create
39	`Decoration.replace({block: true})` decorations hiding everything outside the zoomed range, a
40	`transactionFilter` limiting selection to the visible range, and a breadcrumbs header. Public
41	APIs only. Requires "Fold heading"/"Fold indent" enabled. Proves **zoom/hoisting is achievable
42	with pure public-API CM6** in the standard markdown view.
43	
44	### [obsidian-pro-outliner](https://github.com/mrkhachaturov/obsidian-pro-outliner) (mrkhachaturov)
45	
46	A 2025 unification-fork of outliner + zoom (MIT, credits vslinko) that pushes furthest toward
47	the true-outliner UX and is therefore an important precedent:
48	
49	- Zoom with **compact breadcrumbs** and zoom-out-one-level;
50	- **Mirrors** — Tana-style synced copies of blocks across notes (one-way sync, dashed-bullet
51	  indicator, break-link command; only top-level items mirrorable, nested items fall back to
52	  block links);
53	- **Workflowy-style selection expansion** — selections crossing bullets auto-expand to whole
54	  items with children.
55	
56	Young (v1.0.6, Dec 2025, 21 stars), but demonstrates that node selection, mirrors, and
57	breadcrumbed zoom are all buildable inside the markdown view.
58	
59	### [bullet](https://github.com/kdnk/obsidian-bullet) (kdnk)
60	
61	Active fork of obsidian-outliner (v5.5.9, ~monthly releases) with vim-mode integration and
62	cursor-positioning tweaks. Scorecard on the community directory: Health "Excellent."
63	No fundamental additions over the parent — same flat-text limitations.
64	
65	### draggable-list-items
66	
67	Subsumed: obsidian-outliner (and pro-outliner) ship drag-and-drop natively.
68	
69	### [workflowy-style-outline](https://github.com/springrain1/workflowy-style-outline) (springrain1) — the Option B case study
70	
71	*(Added 2026-07-13 — reviewed after the architecture decisions; it independently attempts our
72	"mode toggle + md↔outline conversion" idea via the custom-view route, so it doubles as a field
73	test of the road we didn't take.)*
74	
75	**What it is.** A Workflowy-style block editor for markdown notes (v2.9.1, ~10k lines,
76	squash-committed as a "fix all Obsidian review issues" clean drop; README additionally
77	advertises license-gated features not present in this snapshot). Rich feature set: per-note
78	toggle to an outline view, zoom + breadcrumbs, multi-selection, drag-and-drop (Alt+drag creates
79	block references with auto-generated native `^block-id`s), slash/tag/link suggestion menus,
80	todo states, mobile toolbar.
81	
82	**How it works** (verified in source):
83	
84	- `WorkflowyView extends FileView` — a **from-scratch block editor**: each node is a raw
85	  `textarea` (edit layer) over a `contenteditable`/`MarkdownRenderer` display layer. Not CM6.
86	- Explicit per-note toggle via command/file menu calling **`leaf.setViewState({type: ...})` on
87	  the active leaf — public API, no monkey-patching**. (Automatic association is simply not
88	  offered; a leaf left in outline view is restored by workspace persistence.)
89	- `OutlineParser` does bidirectional md↔tree conversion; file I/O is manual
90	  `vault.read`/`vault.modify` plus a hand-rolled "anti-race-condition save queue".
91	- A five-module "isolation architecture" (view-state manager, command proxy, event delegator,
92	  runtime validator with periodic health checks) exists solely to keep the parallel editor
93	  from interfering with native Obsidian.
94	
95	**What its costs demonstrate** (each one a predicted consequence of the custom-view route):
96	
97	- **The conversion is lossy and normalizing — the anti-example for our isomorphism
98	  requirement.** The line-regex parser: forces tab indentation and `- ` markers on rewrite
99	  (ordered lists are parsed but re-serialized as bullets), globs *all* consecutive non-list
100	  content (headings, paragraphs, fences, tables) into single opaque blob nodes, misparses
101	  list-looking lines inside code fences, and `.trim()`s away blank-line structure. Toggling a
102	  note in and out of outline view can rewrite the file.
103	- **Rebuilding the editor means rebuilding the platform**: the README's proudest features —
104	  save queue, IME-flicker prevention, incremental mobile DOM patcher, undo manager — are all
105	  re-implementations of things CM6/Obsidian provide for free inside the markdown view.
106	- **Non-list content is second-class**: rendered blobs, not first-class nodes — the exact
107	  compromise our universal mapping (headings/paragraphs as real nodes) is designed to avoid.
108	- Guideline frictions typical of the approach: manual `vault.modify` of the open file,
109	  deprecated `workspace.activeLeaf` usage.
110	
111	**What's genuinely worth learning from it:**
112	
113	1. **Explicit toggle needs no hacks** — `setViewState` to a registered view type on user action
114	   is clean public API; only *automatic* md→custom-view association requires the Kanban
115	   monkey-patch. (Feasibility doc updated accordingly.)
116	2. **Alt+drag → block reference with on-demand `^block-id`** is a lovely interaction that fits
117	   our Q3 decision exactly.
118	3. **Aggregated multi-file outline view** (their "Daily Notes Plus": several notes composed
119	   into one editable outline with lazy mount/unmount) is a compelling future layer — and
120	   easier for us, since our nodes stay in the real editor.
121	4. Their per-block suggestion menus (slash, `#`, `[[`) confirm users expect Obsidian's
122	   full input affordances *inside* outline nodes — which the editor-centric path keeps for free.
123	
124	**Its directory scorecard** (inspected 2026-07-13 on
125	[community.obsidian.md](https://community.obsidian.md/plugins/workflowy-style-outline)) is the
126	first real-world sample of what the automated review measures — Health "Excellent" (activity
127	metrics) but Review **"Risks" with 1,088 flagged issues**, decomposing into:
128	
129	- **~86% is one CSS lint rule**: ~890 × "avoid `!important`" (their from-scratch UI ships a
130	  huge stylesheet fighting Obsidian's), plus "avoid `:has`" (performance).
131	- **The official eslint-plugin-obsidianmd ruleset**: deprecated `activeLeaf` (7×), default
132	  hotkeys (10×), `document` instead of `activeDocument` (24×), `instanceof` instead of
133	  `.instanceOf()` (6×), deprecated `execCommand`/`substr`/`setWarning`, README/manifest name
134	  mismatch — plus strict-TypeScript hits (44 × `any`, unsafe assignments, unhandled promises).
135	- **Supply-chain/build items**: no committed lockfile → *build verification, malware,
136	  obfuscation and network scans all "not available"*; missing GitHub artifact attestations;
137	  release-manifest mismatch; empty release notes.
138	- **Capability disclosures** (not violations, but shown to users): network request calls,
139	  clipboard access, `atob`/`btoa` base64 ("may be used to obscure strings").
140	
141	Notably **absent**: any check for monkey-patching or private-API access — those surface in
142	manual review, not the scans. The scorecard lesson: "Review: Risks" here is mostly lint-grade
143	hygiene, which means a green scorecard is cheaply *designable-in from day 1* (see the
144	feasibility doc's checklist) — and conversely that a red badge on a technically-interesting
145	plugin still poisons user trust on the directory page.
146	
147	## Backlinks-side plugins ("bi-directional outlining")
148	
149	### [influx](https://github.com/jensmtg/influx)
150	
151	Renders backlinks at the bottom of a note as contextual excerpts **organized by the referencing
152	note's bullet hierarchy** — the closest thing to Logseq's structured backlinks in Obsidian.
153	Originally stale/abandoned; picked up by a new maintainer (@semanticdata) and currently
154	maintained. Renders its own panel (doesn't patch the core pane). Read-only excerpts; no
155	in-place editing of the referencing blocks.
156	
157	### [better-search-views](https://github.com/ivan-lednev/better-search-views)
158	
159	Outliner-style context (ancestor breadcrumbs + child items, rendered markdown) injected into
160	core search, backlinks, and embedded queries. **How**: `monkey-around` patches on Obsidian
161	internals — `Component.addChild`, the search result DOM's `addResult`, and
162	`renderContentMatches` — with SolidJS rendering into the patched DOM. The README warns it "may
163	break after an update," and that is exactly what happens periodically. It is the demonstration
164	of *both* the demand for structured backlinks *and* the cost of the private-API route.
165	
166	### [coalesce](https://github.com/bfloydd/coalesce)
167	
168	Newer attempt (inspired by Roam/Logseq): replaces in-document backlinks with a fuller
169	configurable view (aliases, filters, daily-notes orientation). Own view, public APIs, active.
170	Still read-only context display.
171	
172	### Takeaway for the backlinks goal
173	
174	Everyone who patches core panes breaks; everyone who renders their own view survives. Obsidian
175	core already parses every file's list hierarchy into `ListItemCache` (see feasibility doc), so a
176	**first-party-quality structured backlinks pane is buildable on public APIs** — and none of the
177	existing attempts offer *editable* backlink blocks (Roam/Logseq's killer feature), which is an
178	open opportunity.
179	
180	## Gap analysis — what no plugin (or combination) provides today
181	
182	1. **Enforced tree invariants** — a document that *cannot* be structurally corrupted while the
183	   plugin is active (the "true outliner" core).
184	2. **Node-level selection as the primary model** (pro-outliner's expansion is a partial step).
185	3. **A coherent single product** — today the UX is scattered across outliner + zoom + influx +
186	   styling snippets, each with its own settings, gaps, and conflicts.
187	4. **Editable structured backlinks** (transclusion-grade).
188	5. **First-class multiline nodes** with predictable Enter/Shift+Enter grammar.
189	6. **Fold-state persistence** with a deliberate storage story (Obsidian folds are ephemeral
190	   view state; Logseq's `collapsed::` pollution is the anti-pattern to avoid).
191	7. **Clean-scorecard engineering** — several incumbents rely on `any`-casts, globals, or
192	   monkey-patching; none was built with the 2026 automated-review scorecard as a design
193	   constraint.

1	# Obsidian API Feasibility
2	
3	The core question: **can a true-outliner experience be built with public plugin APIs only —
4	no private APIs, no monkey-patching, a clean automated-review scorecard?**
5	
6	Short answer: **yes for the editor-centric architecture; no (today) for a Kanban-style
7	custom-view takeover of `.md` files.** Details below.
8	
9	## The constraint set ("perfect scorecard")
10	
11	As of 2026 Obsidian runs a [Community directory with automated review](https://obsidian.md/blog/future-of-plugins/):
12	every plugin *version* is scanned for security and code quality, and each plugin page shows a
13	**safety scorecard** (with planned disclosure of network/filesystem/clipboard access). Manual
14	review continues for popular plugins. Compliance is checkable locally via the official
15	[`obsidianmd/eslint-plugin`](https://github.com/obsidianmd/eslint-plugin).
16	
17	**What the scorecard empirically measures** (decomposed from a real red one — see the
18	workflowy-style-outline case study in the plugin-landscape doc). Two independent badges:
19	*Health* (activity: readme/license hygiene, commit/release cadence, issue responsiveness,
20	adoption) and *Review* (automated scans of each release). The Review scans are, concretely:
21	(1) capability detection surfaced as user-facing disclosures — vault read/write, network
22	calls, clipboard, base64 usage; (2) dependency vulnerability scan; (3) malware/obfuscation/
23	network-behavior scans gated on **build verification, which requires a committed lockfile**;
24	(4) the obsidianmd + strict-TypeScript eslint rules; (5) CSS lints (`!important`, `:has`);
25	(6) release hygiene (GitHub artifact attestations, release notes, manifest/release
26	consistency). Monkey-patching/private-API use is *not* among the automated checks — it
27	remains a manual-review and fragility concern, not a scorecard line item.
28	
29	**Green-scorecard checklist for this project (day 1):** eslint-plugin-obsidianmd + strict
30	typescript-eslint in CI; zero `!important`/`:has` (style via Obsidian CSS variables and
31	specificity); committed lockfile + GitHub artifact attestations + real release notes in the
32	release workflow; no network/clipboard capability at all (vault-only footprint → near-empty
33	disclosure panel); no deprecated APIs (`activeLeaf`, `execCommand`, …).
34	
35	Relevant hard rules from the [Developer policies](https://docs.obsidian.md/Developer+policies)
36	and [Plugin guidelines](https://docs.obsidian.md/Plugins/Releasing/Plugin+guidelines):
37	
38	- No telemetry, no ads, no self-updating, no code obfuscation.
39	- Security: no `innerHTML`/`outerHTML` with user data — build DOM via `createEl()` etc.
40	- Vault API over Adapter; `Editor` API for active-note edits; `Vault.process()` for background
41	  edits; `processFrontMatter()` for YAML; `normalizePath()`; no `workspace.activeLeaf` access;
42	  register everything for auto-cleanup; no default hotkeys; sentence case UI; no Node/Electron
43	  APIs if mobile is supported.
44	
45	Note: the policies do not *explicitly* ban private-API use — but monkey-patching internals is
46	exactly what the code-quality scan and reviewers look for, it's the community's #1 breakage
47	source, and the project's own bar ("no private APIs or hacks") rules it out regardless.
48	
49	## What Obsidian already gives us (public, documented)
50	
51	This list is the pleasant surprise of the research — the platform provides more outliner
52	primitives than any of the reference apps had to start from:
53	
54	1. **The entire CodeMirror 6 extension surface is official API.**
55	   `Plugin.registerEditorExtension()` accepts arbitrary CM6 extensions: `StateField`,
56	   `ViewPlugin`, `keymap` (with `Prec.highest`), **`transactionFilter` / `transactionExtender`**
57	   (veto or rewrite any document/selection change → this is the enforcement mechanism),
58	   `Decoration.replace/widget/line` (hide syntax, draw node chrome, block widgets),
59	   `atomicRanges` (make bullet markers cursor-atomic), fold service, drop handlers.
60	   obsidian-outliner, obsidian-zoom and pro-outliner jointly prove Enter/Tab grammar, stick-to-
61	   content cursors, subtree hiding (zoom), selection limiting, selection expansion, drag-and-
62	   drop, mirrors, and breadcrumb headers all work through this surface.
63	2. **Core parses list structure for the whole vault already.** `CachedMetadata.listItems` gives
64	   every list item's span and **`parent` pointer** (plus task state) for every markdown file,
65	   kept fresh by the metadata cache — a vault-wide outline index for free. Combined with
66	   `resolvedLinks` (also public), this is everything needed for structured backlinks *without
67	   touching private search internals*.
68	3. **Block identity is native.** `^block-id` IDs, `[[note#^id]]` links, `![[note#^id]]` embeds
69	   are core Obsidian markdown — indexed (`CachedMetadata.blocks`), rendered, hover-previewed
70	   and search-integrated by core. A true outliner can adopt them as its node-reference syntax
71	   and inherit the whole ecosystem's compatibility, instead of inventing `id::` pollution
72	   (Logseq's mistake) or `((refs))`.
73	4. **Folding, Live Preview, `foldInfo`** — native list folding in the editor;
74	   `MarkdownView`/`editor:toggle-fold`; Live Preview already demonstrates syntax-hiding UX.
75	5. **Workspace/view APIs** — `registerView` for our own panes (structured backlinks, breadcrumb
76	   bar via `addStatusBarItem`/view header, etc.), `ItemView`, `MarkdownRenderer.render` for
77	   rendering node content in our panes.
78	6. **Events + `Vault.process`** for cross-file operations (mirror sync, refactors) done safely.
79	
80	## Architecture options
81	
82	### Option A — Editor-centric: CM6 extensions inside the standard markdown view ("recommended")
83	
84	The markdown view stays; the plugin makes it *behave* like an outliner for outline documents:
85	
86	- **Enforcement**: a `transactionFilter` normalizes every transaction — deletions crossing node
87	  boundaries become structural deletions; pastes are re-indented to valid depth; a line can
88	  never lose its bullet while in outline mode; selection changes escalate to node selection when
89	  crossing boundaries (pro-outliner already ships a v1 of this).
90	- **Grammar**: high-precedence keymaps for Enter/Shift+Enter/Tab/moves (proven).
91	- **Node UX**: decorations for bullets/handles/fold chevrons; block-select highlight via line
92	  decorations; zoom via replace-decorations + breadcrumbs (proven by obsidian-zoom).
93	- **Scope control**: extensions are registered globally but no-op unless the file is an outline
94	  document (frontmatter flag / folder / global mode — open question), checked via metadataCache.
95	
96	*Pros*: 100% public API; perfect-scorecard compatible; mobile works; degrades gracefully (files
97	are plain markdown lists everywhere else); interops with the whole ecosystem (backlinks, graph,
98	search, sync, other plugins). *Cons*: we inherit markdown-view constraints — the DOM is CM6's
99	line-based DOM, so some pixel-perfect block UI (e.g., Notion-style hover menus per block) takes
100	more creativity; "hard" enforcement is a filter over a text buffer, which demands a rigorous,
101	well-tested invariant layer (this is exactly the pure tree-ops + property-test discipline from
102	the TUI prototype).
103	
104	### Option B — Custom view: parse md → own block editor (Kanban/Excalidraw pattern)
105	
106	A `TextFileView` renders the outline as a fully custom (e.g., per-node contenteditable/CM)
107	block UI; the file stays markdown on disk.
108	
109	The blocker: **there is no public mechanism to open `.md` files in a custom view.**
110	Kanban does it by monkey-patching `WorkspaceLeaf.setViewState` (confirmed in source: intercepts
111	`state.type === 'markdown'`, checks for its frontmatter key, rewrites to its view type) — a
112	private-API hack, fragile, and contrary to this project's bar. A
113	[2025 forum thread](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/custom-viewer-ui-for-certain-markdown-files/108104)
114	asking for an official alternative got no answer from the team. Alternatives all lose something
115	essential: a custom extension (`.outline`) forfeits metadataCache/links/graph/mobile-editor
116	interop; "open via command" per file is UX friction and re-breaks on every workspace restore.
117	
118	*Nuance discovered later (2026-07-13, from the
119	[workflowy-style-outline](https://github.com/springrain1/workflowy-style-outline) case study —
120	see the plugin-landscape doc)*: the monkey-patch is only needed for **automatic** association.
121	An **explicit, user-invoked toggle** — a command/menu item calling
122	`leaf.setViewState({type: OUR_VIEW})` on the active leaf — is plain public API, and workspace
123	persistence keeps a toggled leaf in that view across restarts. So a scorecard-clean custom view
124	is *reachable*; what remains against Option B is everything else that project demonstrates:
125	rebuilding the editor (per-block textareas, IME/mobile/undo/save-queue re-implementations,
126	an "isolation layer" to avoid breaking native Obsidian) and, in their case, a lossy
127	md↔outline conversion — the anti-example for our isomorphism requirement.
128	
129	*Verdict*: keep as a possible far-future layer (the explicit-toggle variant lowers the API
130	barrier but not the engineering cost); not a foundation compatible with today's goals.
131	
132	### Option C — Hybrid (A + owned side surfaces)
133	
134	Option A for editing, plus our own `registerView` panes where we fully control rendering:
135	**structured backlinks pane** (tree context from `listItems`, editable via `Vault.process` or
136	embedded editors), zoom breadcrumb header, optional outline-wide search/filter view. All public.
137	This captures most of what Option B wanted anyway, without the takeover problem.
138	
139	## Feasibility scorecard per target feature
140	
141	| Target behavior | Public-API path | Confidence |
142	| --- | --- | --- |
143	| Enter/Tab/move/merge grammar | CM6 keymaps (proven in prod) | High |
144	| Structure enforcement | `transactionFilter` invariant layer | High (novel engineering, proven primitives) |
145	| Node selection model | transactionFilter + decorations (pro-outliner v1 exists) | High |
146	| Fold + persistence | native folds; storage decision open (plugin data vs in-file) | High |
147	| Zoom + breadcrumbs | replace-decorations (proven: obsidian-zoom) | High |
148	| Drag & drop | CM6 drop + widgets (proven: obsidian-outliner) | High |
149	| Multiline nodes | md continuation lines + grammar keymaps | Medium-high (careful spec needed) |
150	| Block refs/embeds | native `^id` / `![[#^id]]` | High (core does the heavy lifting) |
151	| Mirrors (synced blocks) | native embeds first; true editable mirrors later | Medium |
152	| Structured backlinks pane (read) | own view + `resolvedLinks` + `listItems` | High |
153	| Editable backlinks | + `Vault.process`/embedded editor per block | Medium |
154	| Mobile | all of the above is CM6/DOM | High (test discipline) |
155	| Perfect scorecard | eslint-plugin from day 1; no `any`-casts into internals | High under Option A |
156	
157	Two honesty notes: (a) `getBacklinksForFile` is **not** public — the public path is
158	`resolvedLinks` + own reverse index (the [backlink-cache](https://github.com/mnaoumov/obsidian-backlink-cache)
159	plugin shows the pattern); (b) even obsidian-outliner reaches for `(editor as any).cm` — we can
160	avoid it almost everywhere by living inside registered extensions (a `ViewPlugin` receives its
161	`EditorView` directly), which should be an explicit engineering rule.
162	
163	## Addendum: the universal isomorphic outline view (post-alignment)
164	
165	The 2026-07-12 alignment (see [04-open-questions.md](04-open-questions.md), Q2) widened the
166	document model from "lists" to the **full markdown block tree** — headings, paragraphs, code
167	fences, quotes, tables *and* list items are all nodes; the outline is a toggleable editor UI
168	over any note, with a lossless (isomorphic) round-trip. Feasibility notes for that vision:
169	
170	- **The parse already exists.** `CachedMetadata.sections` (typed top-level blocks with
171	  positions), `headings` (text + level + position) and `listItems` (span + parent pointer)
172	  together describe exactly this block tree for every file in the vault, maintained by core.
173	  The editor side can parse incrementally from the CM6 document (and/or the bundled
174	  `@codemirror/language` syntax tree) for keystroke-latency accuracy; the cache serves
175	  cross-file features.
176	- **The CM6 architecture is unchanged.** Enforcement (`transactionFilter`), grammar (keymaps),
177	  node chrome (decorations), zoom (replace-decorations) apply to heading/paragraph nodes the
178	  same way they apply to list items. Obsidian's own fold system already treats *both* headings
179	  and list indents as foldable trees — precedent inside the core editor itself.
180	- **The new hard problem is the mapping algebra, not the APIs.** Headings encode depth by
181	  level, lists by indentation, paragraphs by adjacency — three different depth encodings in one
182	  tree. Operations must be closed over "trees expressible as natural markdown" (e.g.
183	  paragraph-under-paragraph has no markdown form → the op must convert, reject, or remap;
184	  h6 bounds heading depth). This is a spec/design problem — precisely what the OpenSpec
185	  explore/design phase is for — not an API gap.
186	- **Prior art**: org-mode is the closest living system (heading tree + plain lists with
187	  subtree promote/demote/move); no Obsidian plugin attempts the unified mapping. Obsidian's
188	  own "fold anything" + `sections` cache suggest the platform was shaped by adjacent thinking.
189	
190	## Bottom line
191	
192	The desired experience is achievable **within the rules** via Option A/C. The differentiating
193	work is not exotic API access — it's (1) a rigorously specified and tested **invariant/
194	enforcement layer** over CM6 transactions, (2) a coherent **node-selection model**, and (3) a
195	**structured backlinks pane** built on the vault-wide list index. The reference precedents
196	(outliner, zoom, pro-outliner, influx) collectively de-risk nearly every primitive; no existing
197	plugin has assembled them into one enforced, coherent product.

Before drawing conclusions, I want real external grounding rather than relying on the postmortem's secondhand summary. I'll dispatch three parallel research agents: one to verify obsidian-outliner's actual technique in source, one on Logseq's rendering architecture (block-based vs. shared-buffer — I suspect this is the crux), and one on broader CM6/editor prior art for cross-kind indentation.

input.prompt:
I'm researching prior art for an Obsidian plugin that needs to draw tree-depth indent guides and node markers across a CodeMirror 6 (CM6) editor buffer. A prior implementation attempt (CSS `padding-left`/`text-indent`/`margin-left` overrides with `!important` on `.cm-line`, driven by a `--to-depth` custom property) broke badly on real content: it fought Obsidian's native list hanging-indent (`text-indent`+`padding-left` pair), needed `!important` to beat cascade, had `em`-vs-`rem` unit bugs (marker size scaling with heading font-size), and collided with Obsidian's fold-indicator chevron when trying to reposition list bullets. I need to deeply verify and expand on what obsidian-outliner (github.com/vslinko/obsidian-outliner) actually does, since a prior research pass (via DeepWiki, high-level only) claimed its vertical indent lines are NOT CSS but a `ViewPlugin` that calls `view.coordsAtPos()`/`lineBlockAt()` to read already-rendered pixel positions and draws absolutely-positioned overlay `<div>`s on top — i.e. it measures post-layout pixels rather than fighting the box model.

Please dig into the actual source (via DeepWiki ask_question/read_wiki_contents on vslinko/obsidian-outliner, and/or by fetching the actual GitHub source files directly — e.g. look for files with "indent", "line", "guide", "style" in the name under src/) and answer with concrete file/line references where possible:

1. Confirm (or correct) the coordsAtPos/lineBlockAt overlay-div claim for vertical indent guide lines. What CSS properties does the overlay actually use (position, left, top, height, width, border/background)? Is it recomputed on every ViewUpdate or something more targeted (viewport-only, docChanged-only)?
2. Does obsidian-outliner (or its forks obsidian-zoom / obsidian-pro-outliner / kdnk/obsidian-bullet) EVER apply any indentation or marker styling to non-list content — headings, plain paragraphs, code fences? Or is every single mechanism strictly scoped to `<li>`/list-item lines only? I need a definitive answer since my working hypothesis is that no existing Obsidian plugin has attempted "unify heading/paragraph/list into one visual indentation language" — only list-internal styling.
3. How does it restyle/replace the native bullet glyph (if at all)? Any `font-size`/`em` tricks, and if so, do they use `rem` or otherwise avoid the "marker size scales with the font-size of whatever line it's on" bug class? Or does it avoid touching bullet size/shape entirely?
4. Does it use `!important` anywhere in its stylesheet? How does it achieve specificity over Obsidian's own `.cm-line` rules if not?
5. How does it handle indent-guide positioning for wrapped/soft-line-broken or multi-line list items (continuation lines)? Does the guide line span the full rendered height of a multi-line item, and how is that height obtained?
6. Does it disable Obsidian's own native `.cm-indent::before` dashes when its own guides are active (a mode class), and how exactly?
7. Any use of `Decoration.widget` with a spacer/inline element (as opposed to CSS padding/margin/text-indent overrides) anywhere in its indentation mechanism — i.e. does it ever "add real rendered width via a widget" rather than override a CSS box-model property?

Report back with concrete, source-grounded findings (cite file paths/line numbers or exact quoted code where you can), organized by the 7 numbered questions above. Flag clearly anything you could not verify from source and had to infer. Keep total length under ~700 words of prose plus any short code quotes.

I'm evaluating whether Logseq's visual "uniform outline" look (every block — regardless of whether its content looks like a heading, a paragraph, or a list item — gets the same fat round bullet and consistent per-level indentation) is a technique that could transfer to a very different kind of editor: a single shared CodeMirror 6 (CM6) text buffer showing raw markdown (Obsidian's Live Preview editor), where headings/paragraphs/list-items are three structurally different markdown encodings sharing one continuous document, each with their own native CSS rendering (headings: distinct typography, no indentation; paragraphs: no indentation, no marker; list items: native hanging-indent via a `text-indent`+`padding-left` CSS pair plus a native bullet/number glyph) — as opposed to Logseq's actual architecture.

My working hypothesis, which I need you to confirm, deny, or refine with real evidence: Logseq's uniform look is achievable *because* every block in Logseq's data model is fundamentally the same kind of thing — a "block" with content and children — and there is no separate "heading" or "paragraph" render-kind at all; something that looks like a heading in Logseq is just a block whose text happens to start with `#` markdown syntax styled larger, but it still gets the exact same bullet, same indent mechanics, same per-block DOM treatment as every other block. If true, this means Logseq never had to solve "make a heading and a list item and a plain paragraph share one indentation language despite being different native constructs" — it sidestepped the problem by giving every block identical structural treatment from the start, at the data-model level, not just the rendering level.

Please research (web search + fetching Logseq's own architecture docs/blog posts + if useful, github.com/logseq/logseq source or its docs for how blocks/pages are rendered) and answer:

1. Is my hypothesis correct? Does Logseq's data model / editor really treat every block identically regardless of whether its content "looks like" a heading vs. a paragraph vs. a list item — i.e. is there truly no separate heading-node or paragraph-node concept distinct from a generic block?
2. Concretely, how does Logseq render a page: is it one big contentEditable/textarea for the whole page (shared buffer, like CM6), or is each block its own independent DOM element/component that's edited in isolation (e.g. a block is static rendered HTML until focused, then swapped for an editable textarea just for that one block, un-swapped on blur)? I need to know whether Logseq's uniformity is fundamentally a consequence of a "one component per block" architecture rather than "one shared buffer with clever CSS."
3. When a Logseq "file-based" (OG) markdown export is inspected, how does a Logseq heading actually get serialized to markdown? Does it use standard `#`/`##` ATX heading syntax, or does everything (including things that look like headings in the UI) get written out as a `-` bulleted list item with heading-level markdown syntax INSIDE the bullet's text? This matters a lot for the "isomorphism with existing plain markdown" question.
4. Any official Logseq engineering blog posts, changelog entries, or design docs discussing WHY they chose per-block components over a shared text buffer, or any tradeoffs they mention (performance, editing complexity, IME issues, etc.)?
5. Briefly (a paragraph is enough): do you know of similar info for Roam Research or Tana's rendering architecture — do they also use one-component-per-block, reinforcing that this is the standard approach among "true outliner" apps, as opposed to a shared-text-buffer editor like CM6/Obsidian?

Report back organized by the 5 numbered questions, with sources cited (URLs). Flag anything you couldn't verify and had to infer or where sources conflict. Keep it under ~700 words of prose.

input.prompt:
I'm researching implementation techniques for a hard sub-problem in a CodeMirror 6 (CM6)-based markdown editor (this is Obsidian's Live Preview editor, extended by a plugin via the public `registerEditorExtension`/`Decoration`/`ViewPlugin` API): making three structurally different markdown encodings — ATX headings (`#`/`##`), plain paragraphs (no native indentation or marker at all), and list items (native CSS hanging-indent via a `text-indent`(negative)+`padding-left` pair, plus a native bullet/number glyph widget) — all visually read as nodes in ONE consistent tree-depth indentation hierarchy, without breaking Obsidian's own native rendering of any of them. A prior attempt tried overriding `padding-left`/`text-indent`/`margin-left` directly via CSS `!important` on every line uniformly by computed tree depth, and it broke: it fought Obsidian's own hanging-indent math, needed increasingly specific selectors, had `em`-vs-`rem` unit-resolution bugs, and collided with Obsidian's fold-indicator chevron when trying to reposition list bullets.

I want to know if there's a fundamentally different, more robust technique than "override the CSS box-model property and hope specificity/units are right everywhere." Please research the following and report back with concrete, sourced findings:

1. **CM6 `Decoration.widget` as a "spacer" technique**: is there known prior art (blog posts, CM6 official examples/docs, GitHub repos) for inserting an inline (not block) widget decoration at the very start of a line's rendered content — an invisible/empty `<span>` with a computed pixel width — to push the rest of the line's content rightward via real layout width, as an alternative to `padding-left`/`text-indent`? Does this actually work as I'd expect (participates in normal inline flow, doesn't fight existing CSS, isn't included in copy/paste text since widgets aren't part of the document's text content)? Any known gotchas?
2. **Emacs `org-indent-mode`**: this is prior art from a completely different editor (Emacs) for a very similar problem — visually indenting a heading's body text to match its outline depth without touching the actual buffer content. It's documented to use `line-prefix`/`wrap-prefix` text properties (which insert virtual, non-buffer characters before each visual line for indentation purposes) rather than any box-model CSS-equivalent. Research exactly how this works (line-prefix vs wrap-prefix, what it renders, performance notes, any known bugs/limitations reported over the years) as a conceptual reference. Is there a rough CM6 equivalent to `line-prefix`, or is the widget-decoration approach in question 1 the closest available analog?
3. **CSS indent-guide techniques from code editors**: research whether there's a well-known CSS technique (used in VSCode, other code editors/CM6 indent-guide plugins/npm packages like a CM6 "indentation markers" extension) for drawing MULTIPLE vertical indent-guide lines per code line (one per ancestor nesting level) via a single element using stacked `background-image: repeating-linear-gradient(...)` or multiple `box-shadow` layers, rather than one DOM pseudo-element per guide line. Find and describe the technique concretely (I want to know if this is more robust/composable than a single `::before` per line).
4. **Silverbullet** (github.com/silverbulletmd/silverbullet) — a CM6-based personal-knowledge-management/note app, conceptually similar to Obsidian but web-based and open source. Does it have any decoration/rendering feature that unifies heading/paragraph/list visual treatment, or per-block "fat bullet"-style chrome? Check its source/docs for anything relevant to cross-kind block decoration in a shared CM6 buffer.
5. Any other CM6-based note-taking/outliner tools (Foam, Dendron, Athens Research (if CM6-based), any "org-mode for CM6" projects) that attempt a similar unification, and how they did it.

Report organized by the 5 numbered questions, concrete and source-grounded (cite URLs), flag anything inferred vs. confirmed. Keep it under ~700 words of prose plus short code/CSS quotes where illustrative.

+// ---- Decorations ------------------------------------------------------------
+
+export interface LineDecorationState {
+  /** `to-node-line` present: a text-flow kind (heading/paragraph/list-item). */
+  hasIndent: boolean;
+  /** `to-node-atom` present: an atom (code/table/quote/callout/html/hr). */
+  hasAtomIndent: boolean;
+  /** Synthetic `::before` marker (paragraphs/headings/atoms without a native one). */
+  hasMarker: boolean;
+  /** Native marker glyph (list bullet/number) restyled in place, no synthetic marker added. */
+  hasNativeMarker: boolean;
+  depth: number | null;
+}
+
+/** Read the rendered CM6 line decorations (class + --to-depth) in document order. */
+export function getLineDecorations(): Promise<LineDecorationState[]> {
+  return browser.execute(() => {
+    // Scope to the active leaf: multiple panes/hidden leaves can each carry
+    // their own `.cm-content`, and an unscoped query would concatenate them.
+    const lines = Array.from(
+      document.querySelectorAll('.workspace-leaf.mod-active .cm-content > .cm-line'),
+    );
+    return lines.map((el) => {
+      const style = (el as HTMLElement).style;
+      const depthRaw = style.getPropertyValue('--to-depth').trim();
+      return {
+        hasIndent: el.classList.contains('to-node-line'),
+        hasAtomIndent: el.classList.contains('to-node-atom'),
+        hasMarker: el.classList.contains('to-node-marker'),
+        hasNativeMarker: el.classList.contains('to-node-native-marker'),
+        depth: depthRaw === '' ? null : Number(depthRaw),
+      };
+    });
+  });
+}
+
+/**
+ * Read each active-leaf line's computed `padding-left`/`margin-left`, in
+ * document order — used to compare actual rendered indentation across node
+ * kinds (e.g. asserting a heading and a list item at the same tree depth
+ * render the same padding-left).
+ */
+export function getComputedLinePaddings(): Promise<
+  { paddingLeft: string; marginLeft: string }[]
+> {
+  return browser.execute(() => {
+    const lines = Array.from(
+      document.querySelectorAll('.workspace-leaf.mod-active .cm-content > .cm-line'),
+    );
+    return lines.map((el) => {
+      const cs = getComputedStyle(el);
+      return { paddingLeft: cs.paddingLeft, marginLeft: cs.marginLeft };
+    });
+  });
+}

---

## Ending Discovery

There's no required ending. Discovery might:

- **Flow into a proposal**: "Ready to start? I can create a change proposal."
- **Result in artifact updates**: "Updated design.md with these decisions"
- **Just provide clarity**: User has what they need, moves on
- **Continue later**: "We can pick this up anytime"

When it feels like things are crystallizing, you might summarize:

```
## What We Figured Out

**The problem**: [crystallized understanding]

**The approach**: [if one emerged]

**Open questions**: [if any remain]

**Next steps** (if ready):
- Create a change proposal
- Keep exploring: just keep talking
```

But this summary is optional. Sometimes the thinking IS the value.

---

## Guardrails

- **Don't implement** - Never write code or implement features. Creating OpenSpec artifacts is fine, writing application code is not.
- **Don't fake understanding** - If something is unclear, dig deeper
- **Don't rush** - Discovery is thinking time, not task time
- **Don't force structure** - Let patterns emerge naturally
- **Don't auto-capture** - Offer to save insights, don't just do it
- **Do visualize** - A good diagram is worth many paragraphs
- **Do explore the codebase** - Ground discussions in reality
- **Do question assumptions** - Including the user's and your own

content.text:
Good — confirms what I suspected: the change shows `"status": "complete"`, all 9 tasks checked off, all artifacts "done." That's the false-confidence problem made visible in tooling terms, not just prose.

Here's the synthesis. I'll go image-heavy since a few structural relationships are easier to see than to read.

## What actually broke, one layer down

The postmortem is honest about the symptoms, but there's a layer underneath it the postmortem doesn't quite say out loud: **the OpenSpec docs and the code are describing two different implementations.**

- `design.md`/`spec.md`/`tasks.md` (as currently written) describe the **D9 walk-back**: paragraphs only, nothing else touched, guide line dropped entirely.
- `decorate.ts`/`decorations.ts`/`styles.css` (as currently on disk) implement **attempt 3**: every kind decorated uniformly, one `padding-left`/`text-indent` pair owned across paragraphs/headings/list-items, `margin-left` for atoms. `decorations.ts`'s own header comment cites "design.md D9/D10" — **there is no D10** in the file. The design doc was never updated past the point where the session pivoted back to universal decoration.

So on top of the CSS bugs, the artifacts drifted from the implementation mid-session, and the change still got marked `complete`. Whatever we decide next, that needs reconciling — otherwise a future session (or future me) reads `design.md` and plans against a scope the code doesn't match.

## The research verdict, compressed

I sent three research agents after this (one of which fanned out into five of its own — the results below are pooled from all of them, all source-grounded, not secondhand summaries):

**obsidian-outliner** (verified against actual source, not just the earlier DeepWiki pass):
- Guide lines are **not** CSS at all. `VerticalLines.ts` measures already-rendered pixels via `coordsAtPos`/`lineBlockAt` and writes them as **inline styles** (`el.style.top/left/height`) onto overlay `<div>`s living outside `.cm-content` entirely. Inline styles from JS always win the cascade — that's *why* it never needs `!important` for positioning (the only two `!important` uses in the whole plugin are unrelated cursor-icon rules).
- Recompute is **debounced** (`docChanged || viewportChanged || geometryChanged`), not on every `ViewUpdate` — our attempt's D4 chose unconditional recompute for a different reason (surfacing the out-of-band mode toggle) but paid a cost precedent doesn't pay.
- Confirmed, categorically: **it never touches heading, paragraph, or code-fence styling.** List-only, by construction.
- Its bullet-dot `em` sizing is safe *only because* `.list-bullet` never exists on a heading line — no cross-font-size collision is even possible. Our marker-size bug (same `::before` rule serving both a heading line and a paragraph line) is a class of bug obsidian-outliner's scoping makes structurally unreachable, not one it solved.
- Wrapped/multi-line item height comes free from `lineBlockAt` (block-level, spans all wrapped visual rows) — no special continuation-line logic needed *because it's measuring, not asserting*.
- **A direct data point on the ask itself**: `vslinko/obsidian-outliner` discussion #53 has someone explicitly requesting heading unification into the outline hierarchy. The maintainer **declined**, citing "One Thing Well." The community's leading, actively-maintained list-outliner plugin was asked for exactly this and said no.

**Logseq** (architecture, not just behavior):

```
 Logseq's data model                    This project's data model
 ────────────────────                   ──────────────────────────
 ALL content = "block"                  heading / paragraph / list-item
 heading-ness = a property               = three distinct native encodings,
   on a generic block                     three distinct native renderers
 (confirmed: no separate                 (each shaped independently by
  heading/paragraph node                  the user's *theme* CSS)
  type in the persisted schema)

 one component per block                 one shared CM6 buffer, one DOM
 (swap rendered ↔ editable                per visual line, native syntax
  on focus/blur)                          decorations layered on top

 → uniform bullet+indent is FREE          → uniform bullet+indent must be
   at the architecture level,               RECONCILED across three
   because there was only ever              independently-themed native
   one render path                          renderers that don't know
                                             about each other
```

And the price Logseq paid for that uniformity: a heading serializes to disk as **`- ## Heading`** — bullet-wrapped, not naked ATX. It gave up plain-markdown fidelity to get uniformity for free. This project's Q2 (`04-open-questions.md`) explicitly requires the opposite — real headings, real paragraphs, isomorphic to *existing* plain markdown, "no change for list-only content." That's not a detail this project could have done differently without abandoning the whole "any existing note becomes an outline" premise — **the harder problem was chosen on purpose**, and Logseq's solution doesn't transfer because it solves a different, easier problem.

**Silverbullet** (the control group — a green-field CM6 note app, zero legacy Obsidian constraints) is the most telling data point: it had every freedom to unify and didn't. Headings/paragraphs/list-items get separate typographic CSS classes (`sb-line-h1`…`sb-line-li`), no shared bullet/marker. Its own docs disclaim being "a 'proper' outliner like e.g. LogSeq." If a from-scratch CM6 tool with no interop debt didn't do it either, the difficulty isn't Obsidian-specific friction — it's inherent to "heterogeneous native block types sharing one buffer."

## So — dead end, or bad attempt?

Neither, cleanly. Reframed:

- **"One visual language, pixel-identical across kinds, via CSS property overrides"** — closer to a dead end. Nobody does this on a shared native-rendering buffer, for a reason: you're reconciling three renderers that don't coordinate, and CSS gives you exactly one lever (specificity + `!important`) that becomes more fragile with every additional kind you fold in.
- **"Some legible, consistent-enough tree signal across kinds, respecting what each kind already does"** — still open, and there's a concrete, differently-shaped technique nobody in this project actually tried: **measure, don't override.**

The thing attempt 3 never did, despite researching it: obsidian-outliner's whole approach is "let native rendering finish, then read the resulting pixels and draw on top." Every bug in the postmortem — cascade fights, `!important`, em/rem, fold-indicator collisions — comes from the opposite move: asserting a value before rendering happens and fighting whatever native CSS also wanted that property. A pixel-measured overlay never has that fight, because it never touches the property native rendering owns.

One more piece of negative evidence worth weighing before you get excited about a clever alternative: I had an agent check whether a `Decoration.widget` "spacer" (real inline content pushing text via layout width, instead of a CSS property override) is viable prior art. It isn't proven anywhere — the community converged on the CSS `text-indent`/`padding-left` pair instead, and there's a confirmed, unresolved gotcha: CM6 wraps every widget in an invisible `.cm-widgetBuffer` that can introduce stray line-wrap points. That's a bad trait for exactly the multiline-continuation case the postmortem flagged as never-tested. Emacs's `org-indent-mode` (the actual prior art for "virtual indent without touching buffer content," via `line-prefix`/`wrap-prefix` text properties) confirms CM6 has no equivalent primitive — no first-row/continuation-row split — so this isn't a "we forgot the right API," it's "the API doesn't exist in CM6." Also worth knowing: `org-indent-mode` has its own long-standing perf and property-collision bugs, so even the "proper" prior art isn't a clean success story.

## A concrete alternative shape, if you want a starting point for prototyping

```
CONTENT INDENTATION                        TREE CHROME (guides, marker)
────────────────────                       ─────────────────────────────
Headings/paragraphs: padding-left is       Draw via measured overlay
purely additive (nothing native to         (obsidian-outliner's technique),
fight) — this part already worked          not CSS pseudo-elements:
in D9, keep it.                              • font-size is fixed & absolute
                                                (no line ever inherited)
List items: NEVER touch text-indent/         • position comes from
padding-left. Add margin-left for              coordsAtPos/lineBlockAt,
*only* the non-list-ancestor portion           after native layout settles
of depth (heading/paragraph ancestors        • debounced recompute, not
above the nearest list root) — the             every ViewUpdate
list's own internal hang math is
untouched, so depth 0 contribution
is byte-identical to native by
construction (a cheap regression
invariant to keep green forever).
```

This directly targets the two worst-looking bugs (marker inflating with heading font-size; list hang corruption) by construction rather than by patch, and it's assembled entirely from techniques with real prior art — nothing speculative.

## Experiments I'd want to see run before writing a real design

In order, each gated on the previous actually surviving real-vault content (not just synthetic fixtures):

1. **Overlay marker only.** One dot per node's first line, positioned via `coordsAtPos`/`lineBlockAt`, fixed absolute size. Falsifiable check: screenshot a heading and a paragraph side by side, diff the marker's pixel bounding box — must be identical.
2. **Additive-only list margin.** Compute non-list-ancestor depth, apply `margin-left` only, assert zero-depth case is byte-identical to outline-mode-off. Test against the exact repro shapes that broke before (checkbox lists, mixed ordered/unordered nesting, list directly under a heading).
3. **Guide lines via the same overlay technique**, specifically exercised against a wrapped/soft-broken continuation line — the one case the postmortem admits was never even screenshotted.
4. *(Only if 1–3 hold up and padding-left still feels fragile on real content)* an isolated spike of the widget-spacer technique against a real wrapped paragraph, purely to see if the `.cm-widgetBuffer` wrap-point bug actually manifests here — go in expecting it might kill the idea.
5. **Fix the verification loop itself**, independent of which technique wins: a fixed, growing screenshot corpus (not per-fix fixtures); one automated check that renders a heading and a paragraph at equal computed depth and diffs *every* decoration-related computed style at once (would've caught both the padding and marker em/rem bugs with one mechanism); `npm run build` actually invoking esbuild or a pre-verify step that forces a real rebuild; a real-vault pass before anything gets marked done again.

Where do you want to push on this? A few live forks I'd flag rather than decide for you:

- Keep `decorate.ts` (the pure depth/marker walk was never the buggy layer) and rebuild only `decorations.ts`/`styles.css`, vs. tear down further?
- Is a *narrower* visual goal — e.g., guides/markers only, no attempt to make list-item indentation absorb heading-ancestor depth — an acceptable fallback if experiment 2 turns out uglier in practice than in theory?
- Do you want the doc/code drift reconciled now (rewrite design.md/spec.md/tasks.md to match whichever direction we pick) before any new prototyping starts, so the next session isn't reading stale artifacts?

okay. good research. first, answering your questions:

* you can do whatever is necessary with the code on this branch. it's a pure WIP. if you want you can stash it and start from scracth, update the proposal/design, etc. I'm not attached to the current code's state, so you can treat it as you see fit.
* I think this is a goo experiment to try: instead of trying to get the consistency with the node markers, skip them for the non-list blocks and just add appropriate indentation and guides, if this it visually clear enough and is convenient to work with, maybe we can settle for such compromise. 
* see the first answer.

next, I want you to flesh out the plans for the different experiments, so that we can try all of them without getting lost in the process and then compare results, learnings, etc.

and finally, a question: since we already parse the markdown blocks into an AST for the structural manipulations, why not follow the approach with the structural editor representation as well? meaning, why not take those parsed blocks, put them into separate DOM nodes, or even separate "block editor" components, or whatever else, where the content of each block is structurally isolated from the rest and rendering of the whole tree can be correct by construction regardless of the content. I understand that this introduces some complexity for keeping in sync with the underlying content: we'd need to sync the edits within a block to the underlying plain markdown. or maybe it's simpler, we have separate editor-windows into concrete parts of the document, arranged in a tree (like tana-mirrors, or the approach we use in https://github.com/laughedelic/obsidian-continuous-journal? check that). I want to understand the feasibility of this general approach better.

Good context to work with. Before I flesh out the experiment plans, I want to properly research your fourth question — the per-block editor architecture — since it's a real fork, not just a variant of what's already ruled out. Let me look at your `continuous-journal` plugin and the general feasibility of embedding multiple live editor surfaces in one Obsidian pane.

We just went through a failed attempt at adding "tree chrome" (indentation, node markers, guide lines) via CM6 (CodeMirror 6) `Decoration`/CSS overrides on a SINGLE shared text buffer — it broke because headings/paragraphs/list-items each have their own independently-themed native CSS rendering in Obsidian's Live Preview editor, and fighting all three with CSS overrides doesn't compose cleanly. Prior research already confirmed: Logseq/Roam/Workflowy achieve visual uniformity by giving every block IDENTICAL DOM/component treatment (one component per block, no distinct heading/paragraph render path at all) — but they pay for it by not preserving plain, idiomatic markdown (Logseq serializes a heading as `- ## Heading`, bullet-wrapped).
